proposals.title: Optimizing Pediatric Antiviral Duration: A Comparative Effectiveness Study of Fixed 5-Day vs. Severity-Adjusted Courses for Influenza and SARS-CoV-2
proposals.motivation: Standardized antiviral courses, like the commonly recommended 5-day regimen for influenza and COVID-19, simplify clinical care but may not be optimal across diverse pediatric populations. Pre-2026, there is limited evidence on whether a single duration is equally effective and safe for young children versus adolescents, or for mild versus moderate disease, leading to potential under-treatment or unnecessary drug exposure. This gap is critical given the themes of antibiotic stewardship and reducing unnecessary testing and follow-up visits in pediatric care.
proposals.hypothesis: A fixed 5-day antiviral course is non-inferior to severity- or age-adjusted duration strategies in achieving clinical resolution by day 14, but is associated with a higher rate of adverse events and subsequent healthcare visits in specific pediatric subgroups.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from a national network of children's hospitals (2018-2024). Pediatric patients (0-18 years) diagnosed with influenza or SARS-CoV-2 and prescribed oral oseltamivir or nirmatrelvir/ritonavir will be included. Using propensity score matching, we will compare outcomes (time to symptom resolution, adverse events, 30-day re-visits) between children receiving a standard 5-day course and those receiving alternative durations. We will employ stratified Cox proportional hazards models and logistic regression, with interaction terms to test for effect modification by age group (<5 years, 5-11 years, ≥12 years) and initial disease severity (mild vs. moderate).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, real-world evidence on the comparative effectiveness of a one-size-fits-all antiviral duration in pediatrics. It will inform more precise, potentially subgroup-specific clinical guidelines, aiming to optimize therapeutic efficacy while minimizing side effects and downstream healthcare utilization.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by this researcher analyzed testing patterns and Mycoplasma pneumoniae epidemiology, this proposal directly evaluates a foundational treatment dogma—fixed-duration therapy—by integrating severity and age as effect modifiers, a nuanced approach not yet applied to pediatric antiviral duration studies.

proposals.title: Population Pharmacokinetics of Oral Beta-Lactam Antibiotics in Children with Mycoplasma pneumoniae and Viral Infections: A Multi-Age Cohort Analysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, pediatric antibiotic dosing often relies on extrapolation from adults or broad age categories, despite known developmental changes in renal and hepatic function. This can lead to subtherapeutic or toxic drug levels, particularly for common oral agents like amoxicillin used for suspected bacterial pneumonia or viral co-infections. The assumption of uniform pharmacokinetics (PK) across pediatric ages lacks rigorous validation in real-world clinical settings, potentially compromising efficacy and safety.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the apparent oral clearance of commonly prescribed beta-lactam antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin, amoxicillin-clavulanate) demonstrates significant, non-linear variation across pediatric developmental stages (infant, toddler, school-age, adolescent), even after adjusting for body size using allometric scaling.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-center study across pediatric hospitals and primary care clinics, enrolling children (0-18 years) prescribed oral beta-lactams for lower respiratory infections. Sparse, opportunistic blood sampling will be performed during standard-of-care follow-up visits. A population PK analysis using non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM) will be employed. Covariates will include age, weight, renal function estimates, and infection type (M. pneumoniae confirmed via PCR, viral infection confirmed via multiplex panel). Model evaluation will use visual predictive checks and bootstrap analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a high-resolution, data-driven model of age-dependent PK for first-line oral antibiotics, directly informing more precise, developmentally tailored dosing guidelines. It will quantify the risk of under-dosing in specific age groups, particularly for infections like M. pneumoniae, and establish a methodological framework for leveraging routine clinical follow-up visits for PK research in children.
proposals.novelty: While population PK studies exist, few integrate specific pathogen data (M. pneumoniae vs. viral) with developmental staging in a real-world ambulatory/hospitalized cohort, and none have leveraged the researcher's established expertise in M. pneumoniae epidemiology and analysis of nationwide ambulatory testing patterns to frame this pharmacokinetic question.

proposals.title: Population Pharmacokinetic Modeling to Optimize Pediatric Dosing of Nirmatrelvir/Ritonavir Using Methods from Pediatric HIV Therapy
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, pediatric dosing for COVID-19 antivirals like nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is often based on simplified weight-band scaling from adult studies, which may not account for developmental changes in drug metabolism and clearance. This can lead to suboptimal drug exposure, risking treatment failure or increased toxicity in children. Dr. Hersh's prior work on infection dynamics and pediatric care underscores the need for precision in pediatric therapeutics to improve outcomes and reduce unnecessary follow-up visits.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying established population pharmacokinetic (PopPK) modeling frameworks from pediatric HIV therapy will identify significant covariates (e.g., age, renal function, formulation) that explain variability in nirmatrelvir/ritonavir exposure in children, enabling more precise, model-informed dosing recommendations than current weight-based approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a PopPK model using nonlinear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM, version 7.5). The structural model will be informed by published adult nirmatrelvir/ritonavir PK and established pediatric HIV protease inhibitor models. We will collate and analyze anonymized pediatric PK data from published studies and available therapeutic monitoring databases (pre-2026). Covariate analysis will evaluate the impact of body size (allometric scaling), age, estimated glomerular filtration rate, and formulation (oral tablet vs. suspension) on key PK parameters. Model validation will use visual predictive checks and bootstrap analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first PopPK model for nirmatrelvir/ritonavir specifically in children, identifying key sources of PK variability. It will generate actionable, model-informed dosing simulations to achieve target exposure linked to efficacy, directly informing clinical practice and future guideline development. The project will also establish a methodological framework for applying HIV pharmacometric expertise to other pediatric antiviral therapies.
proposals.novelty: While PopPK is standard in HIV, its direct application to optimize COVID-19 antiviral dosing in children is novel. The approach innovatively bridges methodological expertise from one pediatric infectious disease domain (HIV) to another (COVID-19), moving beyond simple extrapolation to a rigorous, covariate-driven model tailored to child development.

proposals.title: Rapid Development of a Consensus Guideline for Pediatric Antiviral Use in Community-Acquired Pneumonia Using a Modified Delphi Method
proposals.motivation: The emergence of novel respiratory viruses and evolving resistance patterns of established pathogens like Mycoplasma pneumoniae necessitate timely updates to pediatric antiviral guidance. Current guideline development is often slow, creating a lag between new evidence and clinical practice, particularly in ambulatory and urgent care settings where most children are initially seen. This delay can lead to inappropriate antibiotic use and suboptimal antiviral management.
proposals.hypothesis: A rapid, structured consensus method adapted from emergency medicine can efficiently synthesize pre-2026 evidence to produce a clinically actionable, expert-endorsed guideline for pediatric antiviral use in suspected viral and atypical bacterial pneumonia.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CREDES (Conducting and REporting DElphi Studies) framework for rapid guideline development. A core panel of pediatric infectious disease, emergency medicine, and primary care experts will be convened. Over three iterative rounds, panelists will rate agreement on key statements derived from a pre-scoped evidence review (focusing on viruses and M. pneumoniae) using a 9-point Likert scale. Anonymized feedback and statistical summaries (median scores, interquartile range) will be provided between rounds. Consensus will be defined a priori (e.g., ≥70% of ratings in the 7-9 range). The process will be conducted virtually over a 4-week period.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a rapidly developed, consensus-based clinical guideline ready for pilot implementation. It will provide a validated methodological template for accelerating pediatric guideline updates in response to emerging pathogens. The project directly addresses the translational gap between surveillance data (e.g., on M. pneumoniae hospitalizations) and frontline clinical decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While Delphi methods are established, their application for *rapid* pediatric antiviral guideline development, specifically integrating virology and atypical bacterial pathogens in the ambulatory context, is novel. It transfers and adapts time-sensitive consensus techniques from emergency medicine to a persistent pediatric antimicrobial stewardship challenge.

proposals.title: A Cluster-Randomized Crossover Trial of Rapid Diagnostic-Guided Narrow-Spectrum Initiation in Pediatric Pneumonia: Effects on One-Year Clinical Outcomes and Resistome Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for pediatric pneumonia, often driven by concerns about pathogens like Mycoplasma pneumoniae, contribute to antimicrobial resistance (AMR) without evidence of long-term benefit. Pre-2026, rapid molecular diagnostics for respiratory pathogens are available but not routinely used to de-escalate therapy at initiation. The causal, long-term impact of diagnostic-guided narrow-spectrum initiation on patient outcomes and individual AMR gene carriage remains unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: In children hospitalized with community-acquired pneumonia, a strategy of rapid multiplex PCR-guided initiation of narrow-spectrum antibiotics, compared to usual empiric broad-spectrum care, will result in non-inferior one-year composite clinical outcomes and will cause a significant reduction in the abundance and diversity of resistance genes in the patient gut resistome.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized crossover trial in 20-30 pediatric hospitals or clinical teams. Clusters will be randomized to either an intervention arm (using rapid nasopharyngeal PCR for bacterial/viral pathogens to guide narrow-spectrum initiation within 2 hours) or a control arm (usual empiric broad-spectrum care), with crossover after 12 months. The primary clinical outcome is a composite of treatment failure, pneumonia recurrence, and all-cause mortality within one year post-discharge. Secondary outcomes involve metagenomic sequencing of serial stool samples (baseline, discharge, 1, 6, 12 months) analyzed using tools like HUMAnN3 and MetaPhlAn to quantify AMR gene abundance (via CARD database) and diversity (Shannon index) at individual and cluster levels, using mixed-effects models to estimate causal effects of the assigned strategy.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level causal evidence on the long-term safety and effectiveness of a diagnostic-driven de-escalation strategy at antibiotic initiation. It will directly quantify, for the first time, how an antibiotic stewardship intervention causally shapes the trajectory of the personal and unit-level resistome over a year, linking prescription strategy to molecular AMR reservoirs.
proposals.novelty: The cluster-crossover design eliminates confounding by indication for strategy comparison, while the integration of long-term clinical follow-up with longitudinal, deep metagenomic resistome profiling moves beyond ecological correlations to causal inference at the molecular level within a pragmatic trial.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Ecological Succession Modeling of the Pediatric Respiratory Microbiome to Predict Resistance Emergence Following Antibiotic Therapy for Community-Acquired Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic use in children is a primary driver of antimicrobial resistance (AMR), but the long-term ecological impact of different antibiotic classes on the pediatric microbiome remains poorly understood. Current studies are short-term and fail to model the microbiome as a dynamic ecosystem, missing critical windows for resistance emergence and persistence. This gap limits our ability to prescribe antibiotics that minimize long-term AMR risk while effectively treating acute infections like pneumonia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that antibiotic treatment for pediatric pneumonia acts as a deterministic ecological disturbance, and that the initial antibiotic spectrum (narrow vs. broad) dictates the succession trajectory of the respiratory microbiome, predicting the long-term risk of colonization with resistant bacterial species.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 3-year prospective cohort study of children (2-5 years) hospitalized with community-acquired pneumonia, stratified by initial antibiotic therapy (e.g., amoxicillin vs. broader-spectrum agents). We will collect serial nasopharyngeal swabs at diagnosis, discharge, and 1, 3, 6, 12, 24, and 36 months post-treatment for 16S rRNA and shotgun metagenomic sequencing. Ecological succession will be modeled using generalized Lotka-Volterra (gLV) equations and Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods to infer interaction networks and trajectory stability. Resistance gene carriage will be quantified from metagenomic data and linked to clinical follow-up data on subsequent infections and antibiotic prescriptions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first longitudinal, ecology-based model of AMR emergence in the pediatric respiratory tract following a common antibiotic exposure. It will generate predictive frameworks linking initial antibiotic choice to long-term microbiome resilience and resistance risk. The findings will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship by providing an ecological rationale for spectrum minimization.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely applies formal ecological succession models from conservation biology to the human microbiome over a clinically relevant multi-year timeframe. It moves beyond descriptive snapshots to dynamic, predictive modeling of resistance as an 'invasive species' problem, directly integrating pediatric clinical outcomes with deep longitudinal sequencing.

proposals.title: Causal Effects of Antibiotic Treatment on Clinical Outcomes in Pediatric Pneumonia: A Propensity Score Analysis of Observational Data
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic prescribing for pediatric pneumonia, particularly in ambulatory settings, is common but often occurs without a confirmed bacterial etiology, as highlighted by recent work on urine testing in children with viral symptoms. Observational studies comparing treated and untreated children are confounded by severity and testing practices, leading to biased estimates of treatment benefit. Pre-2026, there is a critical need to apply robust causal inference methods to real-world clinical data to better inform antibiotic stewardship in children.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that after rigorously controlling for confounding using propensity score methods, the estimated causal effect of immediate antibiotic treatment on reducing follow-up visits for pediatric community-acquired pneumonia will be smaller than the unadjusted association observed in raw electronic health record data.
proposals.method: We will analyze a multi-center cohort of children aged 1-18 years diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia in emergency departments and urgent care clinics (2018-2024). The primary exposure is oral antibiotic prescription at the index visit. We will construct a propensity score for treatment using logistic regression with covariates including age, vital signs, radiographic findings, Mycoplasma pneumoniae testing/results, and comorbidities. We will implement 1:1 propensity score matching without replacement and assess balance using standardized mean differences. The primary outcome is a pneumonia-related follow-up visit within 30 days. We will compare outcomes using a conditional logistic regression model in the matched cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more valid estimate of the real-world effectiveness of antibiotics for pediatric pneumonia, directly informing clinical guidelines and stewardship efforts. It will demonstrate a practical framework for applying causal inference techniques to pediatric infectious disease epidemiology using routinely collected clinical data.
proposals.novelty: While propensity scores are established, their application to estimate the causal effect of antibiotics on healthcare utilization in pediatric pneumonia, specifically integrating modern diagnostic patterns for M. pneumoniae and viral testing, is novel. This directly extends the researcher's prior work on testing and follow-up visits into a causal framework.

proposals.title: Implementation and Validation of a Real-Time Learning Health System for Detecting Incident Post-Acute Sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 in Previously Healthy Children
proposals.motivation: By early 2025, post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 (PASC) in children remain poorly characterized, with studies often limited by retrospective design and lack of pre-infection health baselines. There is an urgent need for scalable, real-time surveillance systems to identify emerging conditions like new-onset diabetes or mood disorders following COVID-19, which can inform timely clinical guidance and resource allocation for pediatric care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a learning health system (LHS) using continuously updated electronic health records (EHRs) can detect a significant increase in incident diagnoses of specific conditions (e.g., diabetes, fatigue syndromes) in the 3-12 months following pediatric COVID-19, compared to matched controls, thereby testing the 'inverted comorbidity' hypothesis where infection precedes new chronic disease in previously healthy children.
proposals.method: We will implement an LHS using a common data model (e.g., OMOP) to integrate longitudinal pediatric EHR data from multiple U.S. hospitals. The core analytics engine will employ near-real-time, self-controlled case series (SCCS) and sequence symmetry analysis (SSA) algorithms to flag temporal clusters of new diagnoses post-COVID-19. A rules-based phenotyping layer will identify children with no prior chronic conditions. Detected signals will be fed back into a clinical decision support dashboard to prompt follow-up visits and structured testing, creating an adaptive feedback loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide one of the first operational frameworks for real-time PASC surveillance in pediatrics, generating timely evidence on condition-specific risks. It will directly contribute to refining clinical guidelines for post-COVID pediatric follow-up and testing, moving beyond static cohort studies to a dynamic system that learns from ongoing care.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of near-real-time SCCS/SSA analytics with a closed-loop feedback mechanism into clinical workflows for pediatrics, specifically designed to test the inverted comorbidity hypothesis using pre-infection health baselines—a method not yet widely applied to pediatric PASC surveillance as of 2025.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Machine Learning System for Dynamic Risk Stratification of Pediatric Antibiotic Therapy Failure in Ambulatory and Hospital Settings
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship in children is critical to combat resistance and minimize side effects, yet current practice relies on static initial assessments. This is problematic as conditions like Mycoplasma pneumoniae can evolve, and viral presentations may lead to unnecessary antibiotic starts. There is a lack of tools to dynamically monitor a child's response to narrow-spectrum therapy and signal early failure, risking clinical deterioration and prolonged illness.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid machine learning model that integrates a static baseline risk assessment with a real-time adaptive component, continuously analyzing streaming clinical data, will more accurately predict impending antibiotic therapy failure in children than models based solely on admission or initial visit data.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-component system. The baseline model will be a gradient boosting machine (e.g., XGBoost) trained on historical EHR data (demographics, diagnosis, initial labs, antibiotic choice) to predict a static risk score. The adaptive component will be a recurrent neural network (RNN) with LSTM units, trained on time-series data (vital signs, symptom diaries, follow-up visit notes, repeat point-of-care tests like urine analysis) to update the risk trajectory. The final output is a continuously updated risk score, with alerts triggered upon crossing pre-defined thresholds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for a dynamic clinical decision support tool that moves antibiotic stewardship from a one-time decision to a continuous monitoring process. It will generate novel evidence on the predictive value of temporal clinical data streams for pediatric treatment failure, directly informing guidelines on follow-up and testing.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Hersh et al. has analyzed static factors in pediatric infections and testing, this system uniquely fuses a static baseline model with a real-time adaptive AI component specifically designed for the dynamic nature of pediatric illness, creating a personalized risk trajectory rather than a single snapshot.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Model to Stratify Risk of Clinical Failure in Children with Community-Acquired Pneumonia Prescribed Narrow-Spectrum Antibiotics
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic stewardship in pediatric pneumonia is critical to curb resistance, yet clinicians lack tools to confidently select narrow-spectrum agents like amoxicillin. Current guidelines lead to broad-spectrum overuse due to fear of treatment failure. A data-driven risk stratification tool could safely increase narrow-spectrum use, aligning with Hersh's prior work on *M. pneumoniae* epidemiology and unnecessary testing.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning model trained on demographic, clinical, and microbiological data from children diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia can accurately identify a low-risk subgroup with a high probability of success on narrow-spectrum antibiotic therapy.
proposals.method: We will develop a retrospective cohort using electronic health records from multiple children's hospitals (2018-2024), including children 1-18 years diagnosed with pneumonia and prescribed oral antibiotics. The outcome is clinical failure (escalation, hospitalization, revisit). Features will include age, vital signs, lab results (including *M. pneumoniae* and urine testing data), and prior visits. We will compare multiple pre-2026 algorithms, including logistic regression with regularization (LASSO), random forests, and gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), using temporal validation. Model performance will be assessed via AUC and decision curve analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a clinically actionable risk score to guide initial antibiotic selection in ambulatory care. It directly contributes to antimicrobial stewardship by providing evidence-based criteria for narrow-spectrum therapy. The analysis will also quantify the real-world risk of failure associated with specific pathogens like *M. pneumoniae* in treated populations.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to pneumonia diagnosis, its use for predicting narrow-spectrum therapy success in children is novel. This integrates Hersh's expertise in pediatric pneumonia epidemiology, testing patterns, and clinical follow-up into a predictive model for stewardship, a distinct application from prior diagnostic models.

proposals.title: Mapping the Clinical Niche: A Predictive Model for Narrow-Spectrum Antibiotic Equivalence in Heterogeneous Adult Community-Acquired Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic stewardship in adult CAP is hindered by the clinical heterogeneity of patients and the lack of precision in de-escalation strategies. Current guidelines broadly recommend narrow-spectrum agents like amoxicillin, but their equivalence to broad-spectrum therapy is assumed to be uniform, leading to clinician uncertainty and persistent overuse of broader agents. This contributes to antimicrobial resistance, a critical pre-2026 public health threat.
proposals.hypothesis: Therapeutic equivalence between narrow-spectrum (amoxicillin) and broad-spectrum (e.g., respiratory fluoroquinolones, beta-lactam/macrolide combinations) antibiotics for adult CAP is not universal but is contingent on a patient's position within a multidimensional 'clinical niche' defined by host, pathogen, and environmental factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of a large, multi-center pragmatic trial or observational cohort of adult CAP patients (e.g., data analogous to the CDC EPIC study). The primary outcome is clinical failure at 30 days. We will define the clinical niche using variables including age, comorbidities (COPD, diabetes, heart failure), pneumonia severity index (PSI), local AMR prevalence (from CDC NETSS), and host-response biomarkers (e.g., procalcitonin, C-reactive protein). Using gradient-boosted decision tree models (XGBoost) and interpretable machine learning techniques (SHAP values), we will develop a predictive model to identify subpopulations (specific niches) where narrow-spectrum therapy is non-inferior.
proposals.contribution: This research will move antibiotic stewardship from a one-size-fits-all recommendation to a precision medicine framework for CAP. It will provide a validated, clinically actionable model to identify patients for whom narrow-spectrum therapy is safe and effective, directly reducing unnecessary broad-spectrum use. The 'clinical niche' concept offers a novel paradigm for studying heterogeneity in infectious diseases outcomes.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct adaptation of ecological niche theory to a clinical therapeutic question and the use of interpretable machine learning to move beyond simple subgroup analysis to define complex, predictive clinical niches for antibiotic equivalence.

proposals.title: An Adaptive Control Algorithm for Antibiotic De-escalation in Pediatric Pneumonia: A Randomized Pilot Study
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic overuse and delayed de-escalation in children drive antimicrobial resistance and adverse events. Current de-escalation protocols often rely on static time points or delayed culture results, missing opportunities for earlier, safer therapy. An adaptive, data-driven approach could optimize therapy in real-time, improving stewardship and outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive control algorithm, using daily biomarker feedback and clinical scores to guide antibiotic de-escalation, will reduce total broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure without increasing treatment failure rates in children hospitalized with community-acquired pneumonia, compared to a standard 48-hour de-escalation protocol.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized pilot trial in children (2-18 years) hospitalized with pneumonia at two pediatric centers. The intervention arm uses a predefined algorithm (a modified Recursive Least Squares filter) that processes daily inputs: clinical severity score, procalcitonin trend, and rapid respiratory pathogen PCR results. The algorithm outputs a daily de-escalation probability, guiding therapy adjustment. The control arm follows a standard 48-hour review protocol. Primary outcomes are hours of broad-spectrum therapy and a composite safety outcome of treatment failure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first clinical test of an adaptive control model for antibiotic de-escalation in pediatrics. It will generate a proof-of-concept algorithm and preliminary efficacy data to inform larger trials. The work directly translates signal processing principles into a tangible stewardship tool for dynamic clinical decision-making.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing de-escalation as an adaptive filtering problem, using a real-time, multi-input algorithm to replace static time-based rules. This moves beyond biomarker-guided cessation studies (like procalcitonin for stopping) to focus on dynamic spectrum narrowing.

proposals.title: A Cluster-Randomized Pragmatic Trial of a Dynamic, Dual-Objective Algorithm for Optimizing Antibiotic Selection in Pediatric Community-Acquired Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Pediatric pneumonia management requires balancing immediate clinical efficacy against the long-term ecological threat of antimicrobial resistance (AMR). Current guidelines often rely on static, one-size-fits-all recommendations for narrow vs. broad-spectrum therapy, which may not adapt to local, real-time resistance patterns or cost constraints. This leads to suboptimal individual outcomes and accelerates AMR, a critical pre-2026 public health crisis.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamic, algorithm-driven antibiotic selection strategy that explicitly optimizes for both estimated clinical failure risk and local AMR ecology will yield superior composite outcomes (clinical failure and cost) compared to a static, protocol-driven standard of care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized controlled trial across multiple pediatric hospital sites. Intervention sites will implement a web-based decision algorithm that ingests patient-specific data (e.g., age, severity, local Mycoplasma pneumoniae prevalence from recent surveillance) and hospital-specific inputs (drug costs, local antibiogram). The algorithm uses a multi-armed bandit or Bayesian optimization framework to recommend therapy by dynamically balancing the dual objectives. Control sites will follow a standard pre-specified protocol. The primary outcome is a composite of treatment failure within 30 days and total direct antibiotic cost.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide high-level evidence for the effectiveness of a dynamic, adaptive approach to antibiotic stewardship in a common pediatric condition. It will generate a validated, transparent optimization model that can be calibrated for broader implementation. The study will also produce novel ecological data on how algorithm-driven prescribing influences unit-level resistance patterns over time.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond comparative effectiveness trials of fixed regimens by testing an adaptive, algorithmic system that makes real-time, personalized recommendations based on a formal dual-objective utility function, a concept not yet widely deployed in pragmatic pediatric trials pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Multi-Objective Optimization Model to Balance Cost and Resistance Risk in Pediatric Community-Acquired Pneumonia Empiric Therapy
proposals.motivation: Empiric antibiotic selection for pediatric community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) must balance immediate clinical efficacy, cost, and the long-term public health threat of antimicrobial resistance (AMR). Current guidelines and clinical decisions often treat cost and AMR as secondary considerations, leading to suboptimal stewardship at a population level. This is critical in children, where pathogens like Streptococcus pneumoniae and Mycoplasma pneumoniae are common, and antibiotic exposure is high.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-objective optimization model, parameterized with real-world data on pathogen prevalence, drug costs, and resistance patterns, will identify empiric therapy strategies that achieve near-equivalent clinical coverage at significantly lower cost and reduced selection pressure for resistance compared to current standard protocols.
proposals.method: We will construct a deterministic compartmental model of pathogen susceptibility under antibiotic pressure, informed by national surveillance data (e.g., ABCs, NARMS) and the researcher's prior work on M. pneumoniae. The model will incorporate drug acquisition costs and probabilities of treatment failure requiring follow-up visits. We will employ a Pareto optimization algorithm (e.g., NSGA-II) to explore the trade-off surface between total expected treatment cost (including follow-up) and a composite metric of resistance selection risk, using common oral regimens (amoxicillin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, macrolides) as decision variables.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for evaluating empiric antibiotic choices against dual objectives, moving beyond efficacy-alone paradigms. It will yield specific, data-driven policy insights for pediatric CAP management that explicitly weigh economic and stewardship goals, supporting more nuanced clinical guidelines and formularies.
proposals.novelty: While cost-effectiveness analyses exist, few integrate dynamic resistance selection as a direct, competing objective in pediatric CAP models. This approach combines the researcher's expertise in pediatric pneumonia epidemiology and probabilistic infection modeling to address a core tension in antimicrobial stewardship.

proposals.title: Feasibility and Clinical Impact of Implementing Narrow-Spectrum Antibiotic Guidelines for Pediatric Pneumonia in Low-Resource Hospital Settings: A Multi-Center Pragmatic Trial
proposals.motivation: In low-resource settings, high rates of empiric broad-spectrum antibiotic use for pediatric pneumonia drive antimicrobial resistance and increase costs. Pre-2026 data, including Dr. Hersh's work on *Mycoplasma pneumoniae* epidemiology and urine testing patterns, suggests a significant proportion of cases may be viral or caused by pathogens treatable with narrow-spectrum agents like amoxicillin. However, the feasibility and outcomes of implementing restrictive, pathogen-directed guidelines in hospitals with limited diagnostic capacity are unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pragmatic intervention combining a clinical decision support algorithm with targeted point-of-care testing for *Mycoplasma pneumoniae* and urinary antigens will enable safe implementation of narrow-spectrum antibiotic guidelines, reducing broad-spectrum use by ≥30% without increasing treatment failure rates in hospitalized children.
proposals.method: We will conduct a stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial across 8-10 hospitals in 2-3 low- and middle-income countries. The intervention is a two-part algorithm: 1) For children meeting clinical pneumonia criteria, perform rapid urine antigen testing (for *S. pneumoniae* and *L. pneumophila*) and oral swab PCR for *M. pneumoniae*. 2) Based on results and severity markers, recommend narrow-spectrum therapy (amoxicillin) or continued broad-spectrum. Primary outcomes are days of broad-spectrum therapy and 30-day treatment failure (re-admission, escalation). Analysis will use mixed-effects regression models adjusting for time, cluster, and patient covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust evidence on the real-world effectiveness of a diagnostic-guided, narrow-spectrum strategy for pediatric pneumonia in low-resource hospitals. It will quantify the potential reduction in broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure and identify key barriers (e.g., test availability, clinician adherence) to implementation, directly informing WHO and national antimicrobial stewardship guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have assessed guideline implementation in high-resource settings, this trial uniquely integrates specific, feasible point-of-care diagnostics (informed by recent *M. pneumoniae* epidemiology) into a pragmatic algorithm designed for constraints typical of low-resource hospitals, moving beyond theoretical recommendations.

proposals.title: Modeling the Preventable Burden of Pediatric Bacterial Upper Respiratory Infections: A Simulation Study to Guide Vaccine and Probiotic Development
proposals.motivation: Despite efforts to promote judicious antibiotic use, bacterial upper respiratory infections (URIs) remain a leading cause of pediatric ambulatory visits and antibiotic prescriptions, contributing to antimicrobial resistance. A paradigm shift from reactive treatment to primary prevention is needed. This study leverages recent national data on pediatric URI epidemiology to quantify the potential impact of preventive strategies, aligning with growing pre-2026 interest in next-generation pneumococcal vaccines and microbiome-based interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of pediatric bacterial URI visits, particularly those involving Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenzae, are attributable to a subset of serotypes or pathobiont states that could be targeted by novel vaccines or probiotics, and that this preventable burden can be accurately estimated using longitudinal healthcare data.
proposals.method: We will construct an agent-based simulation model parameterized with pre-2026 data, including URI visit rates from national ambulatory datasets (e.g., NAMCS) and serotype/species distribution from surveillance studies (e.g., ABCs). The model will simulate a pediatric cohort over a 5-year period, incorporating known risk factors for bacterial URI. We will use a competing-risks survival analysis framework, informed by Dr. Hersh's prior work on infection probability functions, to estimate the baseline incidence of preventable infections. The model will then test intervention scenarios, varying vaccine efficacy against non-vaccine serotypes and probiotic colonization resistance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, model-based estimates of the preventable fraction of pediatric bacterial URIs, offering a crucial evidence base for prioritizing research into specific preventive modalities. It will directly inform clinical trial design and public health investment by identifying the key serotypes or microbial targets with the greatest potential population impact.
proposals.novelty: While existing research focuses on diagnosing and treating bacterial URIs, this study inverts the focus to model primary prevention at a population level. It uniquely integrates detailed serotype epidemiology with healthcare utilization patterns to simulate the effect of interventions not yet widely deployed, moving beyond descriptive surveillance to predictive modeling for prevention.

proposals.title: Development and Randomized Controlled Trial of a Pictorial Communication Aid to Reduce Inappropriate Antibiotic Prescribing for Pediatric Viral Respiratory Infections in Ambulatory Care
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic overprescription for pediatric viral infections drives antimicrobial resistance and adverse drug events. Pre-2026 data, including Hersh's work on *Mycoplasma pneumoniae* and national analyses of ambulatory testing, shows high rates of testing and treatment for viral syndromes, indicating a critical need for effective clinician-patient communication to support antibiotic stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: Providing clinicians with a structured, pictorial communication aid that visually explains the lack of antibiotic efficacy against viruses and outlines symptomatic management will significantly reduce the rate of inappropriate antibiotic prescriptions for pediatric upper respiratory infections compared to usual care.
proposals.method: We will design a paper-based pictorial aid using iterative, user-centered design with pediatricians and parents. The aid will use simple graphics to contrast bacterial and viral infections, explain test results (e.g., rapid viral panels, urine testing), and show timelines for viral recovery. We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized controlled trial across 20-30 pediatric primary care clinics. Clinics will be randomized to receive the communication tool or continue usual care. The primary outcome is the antibiotic prescription rate for children (1-17 years) diagnosed with viral upper respiratory infection, bronchitis, or influenza, analyzed using generalized linear mixed models adjusting for clinic-level clustering.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a rigorously tested, scalable communication tool directly applicable to ambulatory practice. It will provide high-quality evidence on the effectiveness of visual aids in changing prescribing behavior, contributing to the implementation science of outpatient antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.novelty: While communication strategies exist, this proposal integrates specific visual explanations of diagnostic test results (a focus of Hersh's prior work) into a concrete tool and tests it in a real-world RCT, moving beyond survey-based studies to measure actual prescribing outcomes.

proposals.title: A Cluster-Randomized Trial of Audit and Feedback to Reduce Inappropriate Oral Antibiotic Prescribing for Pediatric Acute Respiratory Tract Infections in Ambulatory Care
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic overprescription in children, particularly for viral respiratory infections, drives antimicrobial resistance and adverse drug events. Pre-2026 data, including Dr. Hersh's work on national ambulatory visits and Mycoplasma pneumoniae, shows high rates of testing and prescribing in pediatric care. Systematic quality improvement strategies are needed to translate evidence into practice and reduce unnecessary antibiotic use.
proposals.hypothesis: Providing structured, peer-benchmarked audit and feedback on antibiotic prescribing rates to pediatric clinicians will significantly reduce the proportion of acute respiratory tract infection visits resulting in an inappropriate oral antibiotic prescription compared to usual care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial among pediatric primary care practices. Using electronic health record data, we will calculate a baseline antibiotic prescription rate for viral ARTI visits (e.g., bronchiolitis, viral URI) for each clinician. Intervention arm clinicians will receive quarterly, web-based audit and feedback reports displaying their individual rate, a de-identified peer benchmark, and evidence-based guidelines. The primary outcome is the change in prescription rate from baseline to 12-month follow-up, analyzed using generalized linear mixed models adjusted for practice clustering and patient case-mix.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, real-world evidence on the effectiveness of a scalable audit and feedback intervention specifically tailored to pediatric ambulatory antibiotic stewardship. It will identify key moderators of success (e.g., practice size, baseline rate) and generate a replicable framework for benchmarking and feedback using routine clinical data.
proposals.novelty: While audit and feedback is a known method, its application is novel in this specific context: it integrates pediatric-specific diagnosis codes, benchmarks against peer clinicians in community practice (not just guidelines), and leverages the researcher's expertise in national ambulatory patterns and respiratory pathogen epidemiology to define inappropriate prescribing.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Real-Time National Surveillance System for Pediatric Antibiotic Prescriptions for Upper Respiratory Infections
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic overprescription for pediatric upper respiratory infections (URIs), which are predominantly viral, is a major driver of antimicrobial resistance. Prior work, such as Hersh's analysis of urine testing, highlights the frequency of low-value testing and treatment in ambulatory care. A national, real-time surveillance system is urgently needed to quantify the problem, identify geographic and practice-level variation, and inform stewardship interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a scalable surveillance system, built on federated learning from electronic health record (EHR) data, can accurately estimate national and regional rates of pediatric antibiotic prescribing for URIs in near real-time, revealing significant and actionable prescribing heterogeneity.
proposals.method: We will design a distributed surveillance architecture using a federated learning framework (e.g., TensorFlow Federated) to analyze de-identified EHR data from a national network of pediatric hospitals and large care systems. The system will use natural language processing (BERT-based models) on clinical notes and structured ICD-10/CPT codes to identify URI visits and link them to oral antibiotic prescriptions (J-codes). A central coordinating server will aggregate encrypted model updates to compute prescription probabilities and generate spatiotemporal heatmaps without transferring raw patient data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated, real-time model for national pediatric antibiotic prescription surveillance for URIs. It will provide public health officials with a dashboard to monitor trends and target stewardship efforts. The federated approach sets a new standard for privacy-preserving public health surveillance.
proposals.novelty: While prescription databases exist, a real-time, pediatric-specific system focused on URI diagnoses is novel. The application of federated learning to this public health problem, building on methods from distributed COVID-19 risk modeling, represents a significant technical advance over static, aggregated data reports.

proposals.title: A Cluster-Randomized Trial of EHR-Based Nudges to Improve First-Line Antibiotic Prescribing for Pediatric Community-Acquired Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Inappropriate antibiotic prescribing for pediatric respiratory infections, such as community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), drives antimicrobial resistance and adverse drug events. Despite guidelines recommending amoxicillin as first-line therapy for CAP, broad-spectrum antibiotic use remains high in both inpatient and outpatient settings, as evidenced by recent surveillance of *Mycoplasma pneumoniae* and other pathogens. Behavioral economics offers a scalable, low-cost tool to improve guideline adherence without restricting prescriber autonomy.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a set of choice architecture nudges within the electronic health record (EHR) will significantly increase the proportion of first-line antibiotic prescriptions for pediatric CAP compared to usual care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial across a network of pediatric hospitals and affiliated primary care clinics. Intervention sites will receive a bundled nudge intervention in their EHR: 1) making amoxicillin the default selection in order sets for CAP, 2) displaying a peer-comparison feedback message showing the site's rate of first-line prescribing, and 3) requiring a brief justification for deviating from first-line therapy. Control sites will have standard order sets. The primary outcome will be the proportion of CAP encounters where first-line therapy is prescribed, analyzed using generalized linear mixed models adjusted for clustering.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, real-world evidence on the efficacy of EHR nudges for antibiotic stewardship in children, a population with distinct diagnostic and prescribing challenges. It will directly inform health system policies on implementing low-cost behavioral interventions to combat antimicrobial resistance and improve the quality of ambulatory and hospital care.
proposals.novelty: While nudges have been tested in adult populations, their application is novel in pediatrics, where diagnostic uncertainty (e.g., viral vs. bacterial, typical vs. atypical pneumonia) is high. The intervention integrates multiple behavioral principles (defaults, social norms, accountability) specifically tailored to the complex context of pediatric respiratory illness.

proposals.title: Mapping Peer Influence Networks in Pediatric Antibiotic Prescribing: A Social Network Analysis of Ambulatory Visits for Respiratory Illness
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic overprescribing in children, particularly for viral respiratory infections, remains a critical driver of antimicrobial resistance. Prior work by this researcher has quantified testing patterns and infection dynamics, but the social and professional factors influencing prescriber behavior are poorly understood. Understanding how peer networks shape prescribing decisions is essential for designing effective, targeted stewardship interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pediatric prescribers within the same hospital or care network exhibit correlated antibiotic prescribing rates for common respiratory diagnoses, and that central prescribers within these social-professional networks exert a measurable influence on the prescribing habits of their peers.
proposals.method: We will construct a directed, weighted prescriber network using a large, pre-2026 national claims database of pediatric ambulatory visits. Nodes are individual prescribers. Edges will be defined by shared patient visits (a proxy for professional connection) and weighted by co-attendance frequency. We will calculate network metrics (e.g., centrality, clustering coefficient) for each prescriber. Using multivariable regression, we will test the association between a prescriber's network position and their antibiotic prescribing rate for acute respiratory tract visits, adjusting for patient and practice characteristics. The analysis will specifically examine visits for conditions where *Mycoplasma pneumoniae* and viral pathogens are common.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, quantitative map of peer influence structures in pediatric antibiotic prescribing. It will identify key influencer prescribers who could be leveraged for stewardship programs. The findings will shift the focus of stewardship from individual education to network-informed, social diffusion strategies.
proposals.novelty: While social network analysis has been applied to physician behavior in other contexts, its application to pediatric antibiotic prescribing using real-world, population-scale administrative data to model peer influence is novel. This moves beyond characterizing individual prescriber factors to model the system of professional relationships driving practice patterns.

proposals.title: Cost-Effectiveness of Outpatient Parenteral Antimicrobial Therapy (OPAT) for Pediatric Pneumonia: A Microsimulation Model Comparing Hospital-Based, Skilled Nursing Facility, and Home-Based Care
proposals.motivation: Outpatient Parenteral Antimicrobial Therapy (OPAT) is a critical strategy for managing serious infections like Mycoplasma pneumoniae in children, aiming to reduce hospital length-of-stay and associated costs. However, pre-2026 evidence on the comparative cost-effectiveness of different OPAT delivery models (e.g., hospital-based infusion centers, skilled nursing facilities, home-based care) for pediatric populations is sparse. This gap hinders evidence-based decisions by clinicians, payers, and health systems seeking to optimize resource allocation and child-centered care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that home-based OPAT is the most cost-effective delivery model for stable pediatric patients with community-acquired pneumonia requiring prolonged intravenous antibiotics, compared to hospital-based or skilled nursing facility models, when accounting for direct medical costs, caregiver burden, and clinical outcomes including treatment failure and re-hospitalization.
proposals.method: We will develop a state-transition microsimulation model in R, using a healthcare system perspective and a 30-day time horizon. Model parameters (e.g., transition probabilities for complications, re-admission, resource use, and costs) will be derived from retrospective analyses of pediatric hospital databases (e.g., PHIS/KID) and the researcher's prior work on pediatric pneumonia hospitalizations and ambulatory testing. Costs will be obtained from CMS and HCUP databases. We will perform deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity analyses (using Monte Carlo simulation with 10,000 iterations) to assess model robustness and identify key cost drivers.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first pediatric-specific cost-effectiveness model for OPAT program design, quantifying trade-offs between cost, family burden, and clinical risk. The results will offer a decision-analytic framework for hospitals and insurers to evaluate investments in OPAT infrastructure and patient selection criteria, directly informing value-based care strategies for pediatric infectious diseases.
proposals.novelty: While adult OPAT cost-effectiveness has been studied, this model uniquely integrates pediatric-specific epidemiology (e.g., Mycoplasma pneumoniae), caregiver time costs, and ambulatory follow-up patterns derived from nationwide visit data, moving beyond simple cost-comparisons to a dynamic simulation of clinical and economic outcomes.

proposals.title: Variation in Inappropriate Antibiotic Prescribing for Pediatric Respiratory Illnesses Across Emergency Departments: A National Analysis
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic stewardship is critical in pediatrics to combat resistance and avoid adverse drug events. While pediatric emergency departments (EDs) are specialized, it is unknown if this inherently optimizes prescribing or if significant, modifiable variation persists. This study addresses a key gap by quantifying ED-level and provider-level variation in inappropriate prescribing for conditions like viral URIs and bronchitis, where antibiotics are not indicated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that substantial, statistically significant variation in inappropriate antibiotic prescribing rates exists across pediatric EDs and among providers within EDs, independent of patient case-mix, and that high-prescribing outliers can be identified.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using national administrative claims data (e.g., IBM MarketScan, 2018-2024) for pediatric ED visits. The primary outcome is an antibiotic prescription within 3 days for a visit with a primary diagnosis of a non-bacterial respiratory condition. We will use hierarchical logistic regression models with random intercepts for ED and provider, adjusting for patient demographics, comorbidities, and visit characteristics. High-outlier EDs and providers will be identified using the Estimated Bayesian Posterior probability from the random effects model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first national, multi-year benchmark of inappropriate antibiotic prescribing in pediatric EDs, moving beyond facility-level audits. It will identify specific high-outlier institutions and providers, offering actionable targets for focused stewardship interventions. The methodological approach demonstrates how to robustly adjust for case-mix when comparing performance across healthcare settings.
proposals.novelty: Prior work has focused on prescribing in general EDs or outpatient clinics; this specifically tests the assumption that pediatric specialization equates to optimal stewardship. The application of hierarchical modeling to identify outlier providers within the unique, high-acuity ED context is novel for antibiotic use research.

proposals.title: Diagnosis-Level Variation in Antibiotic Prescribing for Pediatric Infections: A Comparative Analysis of Pediatric and General Emergency Departments
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic overuse in children drives resistance and adverse events. While pediatric emergency departments (PEDs) are presumed to follow pediatric-specific guidelines more closely, the magnitude and specific diagnostic drivers of prescribing differences compared to general EDs (GEDs) are poorly quantified at a granular level. This gap limits targeted stewardship interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant differences in antibiotic prescribing rates between PEDs and GEDs are concentrated in a subset of common pediatric infections (e.g., acute otitis media, pneumonia, pharyngitis) where guideline interpretation and diagnostic uncertainty vary, rather than being uniform across all diagnoses.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using a national administrative claims database (e.g., IBM MarketScan, 2018-2024). Pediatric ED visits (age <18 years) will be identified and stratified by ED type (pediatric vs. general). For each visit, we will extract primary diagnosis codes (ICD-10-CM) and associated antibiotic prescriptions. We will calculate diagnosis-specific prescribing rates and use multivariable logistic regression, adjusting for age, comorbidities, and region, to estimate the adjusted odds ratio of antibiotic prescribing in GEDs versus PEDs for each top diagnosis. The analysis will specifically model pathogens like *Mycoplasma pneumoniae* and *Streptococcus pneumoniae*.
proposals.contribution: This study will identify the specific diagnoses where stewardship efforts in general EDs could have the greatest impact, moving beyond aggregate comparisons. It will provide actionable, diagnosis-level evidence to inform tailored educational and clinical decision support tools for non-pediatric emergency settings.
proposals.novelty: Prior work often compares aggregate prescribing or focuses on broad conditions. This diagnosis-level, pathogen-aware analysis within a national cohort of ED visits pinpoints precise clinical scenarios of divergent practice, building on Hersh's recent work on *M. pneumoniae* and diagnostic testing in ambulatory pediatrics.

proposals.title: Real-Time Monitoring of Pediatric Emergency Department Antibiotic Prescribing Using Statistical Process Control Charts
proposals.motivation: Inappropriate antibiotic prescribing in pediatric emergency departments (EDs) drives antimicrobial resistance and adverse drug events. Current quality improvement efforts rely on retrospective audits, which delay feedback and limit timely intervention. Real-time, data-driven monitoring tools are urgently needed to support stewardship at the point of care.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing statistical process control (SPC) charts on electronic health record data will enable the real-time detection of significant deviations in pediatric ED antibiotic prescribing rates, allowing for prompt investigation and corrective action.
proposals.method: We will apply SPC methodology, specifically p-charts for attribute data, to monitor the daily proportion of pediatric ED visits resulting in an oral antibiotic prescription. Data will be extracted in near-real-time from the ED electronic health record. Baseline prescribing rates will be established using historical data (e.g., 2018-2023), and control limits (upper and lower) will be calculated. The system will flag days where prescribing rates exceed control limits, triggering a structured review by the stewardship team.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, automated framework for continuous quality monitoring of antibiotic use in a high-volume pediatric setting. It will demonstrate the feasibility of adapting industrial quality control techniques to healthcare delivery, moving stewardship from periodic audit to real-time process control. The work will generate a replicable model for other institutions.
proposals.novelty: While SPC is established in manufacturing, its application to real-time antibiotic stewardship in pediatric emergency care is novel. This approach moves beyond descriptive analytics to a dynamic monitoring system that differentiates common-cause variation from special-cause signals requiring intervention.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Prevalence and Determinants of Socially-Driven Pediatric Hospitalizations: A Multi-Hospital Cohort Study Integrating Structured and Unstructured Data
proposals.motivation: Hospitalizations for non-acute medical needs, such as social isolation or lack of safe discharge options, represent a critical inefficiency in pediatric healthcare, consuming resources and potentially causing iatrogenic harm. Prior work, including analyses of ambulatory testing and *Mycoplasma pneumoniae* hospitalizations, highlights the need to distinguish clinical from non-clinical drivers of care. A standardized, evidence-based framework to quantify this phenomenon is urgently needed to inform hospital policy and identify gaps in community-based support systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of pediatric hospitalizations, particularly for conditions like viral syndromes or mild pneumonia, are primarily driven by non-clinical social factors, and that these cases can be reliably identified through a composite metric derived from electronic health record data.
proposals.method: We will apply a rule-based operational framework to a multi-hospital cohort of children (<18 years) admitted 2018-2024. The framework integrates structured data (e.g., short length of stay, minimal therapeutic interventions, low-complexity diagnosis) with natural language processing (NLP) of clinical notes using pre-2026 models (e.g., cTAKES or a fine-tuned BERT variant) to flag keywords related to social needs, isolation, and custody. Cases will be classified using a probabilistic score, validated via manual chart review. Demographics, costs, and seasonal trends will be analyzed.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, quantitative estimate of the prevalence and associated costs of socially-driven pediatric hospitalizations in the U.S. It will deliver a validated, operational tool for hospitals to audit admission necessity and will identify specific patient demographics and community care gaps associated with these potentially avoidable admissions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined testing patterns and infection-specific hospitalizations, this study uniquely develops a standardized, data-driven framework to directly quantify the non-clinical drivers of admission, moving beyond anecdote to measurable prevalence by integrating structured EHR metrics with NLP analysis of social determinants documented in clinical notes.

proposals.title: Disentangling Clinical Severity from Social Drivers of Pediatric Hospitalization for Respiratory Infections: A Retrospective Cohort Analysis
proposals.motivation: Hospitalization rates for pediatric respiratory infections are a key metric for disease burden and healthcare strain. However, pre-2026 research, including Hersh's work on *M. pneumoniae* and viral testing, suggests admissions may be influenced by non-clinical factors like the need for isolation in crowded homes or lack of outpatient follow-up, especially post-COVID-19. This conflation obscures true disease severity trends and may lead to misallocation of inpatient resources.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant proportion of pediatric hospitalizations for common respiratory pathogens (e.g., *M. pneumoniae*, influenza, RSV) are primarily driven by social or isolation needs rather than objective clinical severity, and these 'social admissions' can be predicted using clinical and demographic variables available at emergency department presentation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from 2018-2024 for children aged 1-17 years hospitalized with a primary diagnosis of community-acquired pneumonia or bronchiolitis. We will define a 'low-severity' clinical phenotype using a pre-2026 validated pediatric severity index (e.g., PSI/pSOFA components), vital sign stability, and lack of intensive interventions within 24 hours of admission. Using multivariable logistic regression, we will model the probability of a 'low-severity' admission as a function of social variables (crowding index, caregiver constraints, distance from hospital) and pathogen type, adjusting for age and comorbidities. Sensitivity analyses will use machine learning (LASSO regression) to identify key predictive feature sets.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative estimate of 'social admissions' for pediatric respiratory infections in the post-COVID era, directly challenging the assumption that hospitalization volume equates to disease severity. It will generate a pragmatic clinical prediction rule to help emergency departments identify children who may be safely managed in alternative settings with enhanced outpatient follow-up, as explored in Hersh's prior work on ambulatory visits.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted social determinants of health, this study explicitly operationalizes and quantifies 'social admission' as a distinct category from clinical severity for common infections, leveraging detailed pathogen data and pre-2026 severity scores to create a disentangled model.

proposals.title: Causal Inference to Identify Latent Severity and Estimate Antibiotic Treatment Effects in Children Discharged from the Emergency Department
proposals.motivation: In pediatric emergency care, discharge is often used as a proxy for mild illness, but this assumption may be flawed due to diagnostic uncertainty, leading to misclassification bias. This bias obscures the true effect of antibiotic prescriptions on post-discharge outcomes like return visits, a critical issue given ongoing concerns about antibiotic stewardship and outcomes for pathogens like Mycoplasma pneumoniae. Existing observational studies, such as those analyzing nationwide ambulatory visits, often pool all discharged patients, potentially masking heterogeneous treatment effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within a cohort of children discharged from the ED with respiratory infections, a latent high-severity subgroup exists, and that causal inference methods can identify this subgroup and yield less biased estimates of antibiotic effectiveness in preventing return visits and subsequent hospitalization.
proposals.method: We will analyze observational ED data (e.g., from the Pediatric Health Information System, PHIS) for children with respiratory infections discharged between 2018-2024. Using a causal inference framework, we will first employ an instrumental variable (e.g., physician prescribing preference) or g-methods (like g-computation) to test the discharge=mild-illness assumption by modeling latent severity. Subsequently, within the identified higher-severity stratum, we will apply targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) or propensity score-based methods to estimate the average treatment effect of antibiotics on 7- and 30-day return visits and hospitalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal methodological framework for quantifying misclassification bias in common pediatric ED outcomes research. It will generate novel, severity-stratified estimates of antibiotic treatment effects on return visits, directly informing clinical decision-making for discharged children who are at higher risk for poor outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While causal inference is used in pharmacoepidemiology, its application to formally test and correct for outcome misclassification (discharge status) in pediatrics is novel. This approach moves beyond simple stratification to probabilistically identify a latent high-risk cohort for targeted effect estimation.

proposals.title: Post-Discharge Morbidity in Children with Respiratory Illness: A Prospective Cohort Study of Emergency Department Discharges and Subsequent Healthcare Utilization
proposals.motivation: Emergency department (ED) discharge decisions for children with respiratory symptoms often assume mild, self-limiting disease. However, prior work by Hersh et al. on *Mycoplasma pneumoniae* and viral testing indicates significant infection burdens in ambulatory settings. The clinical assumption that ED discharge equates to mild illness may be flawed, potentially leading to unrecognized progression and unsafe discharges, especially with evolving pathogens and antibiotic stewardship concerns.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant proportion of children discharged from the ED with acute respiratory illness will experience substantial post-discharge morbidity, defined as escalation of care (hospital admission, urgent care/ED revisit) or prolonged symptoms, within 7-14 days, and this risk is associated with specific clinical and microbiological factors (e.g., pathogen, antibiotic prescription at discharge).
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center prospective cohort study of children (1-17 years) discharged from the ED with a respiratory diagnosis. We will collect standardized clinical data, obtain oral/nasal swabs for multiplex PCR pathogen testing (including *M. pneumoniae*, SARS-CoV-2, influenza, RSV), and conduct structured telephone follow-up at 3, 7, and 14 days post-discharge. The primary outcome is a composite of ED revisit leading to admission or urgent care visit for worsening symptoms. We will use multivariable logistic regression, modeling time-to-event using methods analogous to Hersh's prior work on infection probability, to identify predictors of post-discharge morbidity.
proposals.contribution: This study will quantify the risk of significant post-ED morbidity in a population presumed to be low-risk, directly testing a foundational clinical assumption. It will identify high-risk clinical and microbiological profiles, informing discharge criteria and the potential role of targeted testing (e.g., for *M. pneumoniae*) or closer follow-up plans to improve pediatric outcomes and resource use.
proposals.novelty: While revisits are tracked, this prospectively links detailed, etiology-specific pathogen data from the index visit with structured symptom and outcome follow-up, moving beyond retrospective claims data to actively test the 'mild disease' assumption and identify modifiable discharge factors.

proposals.title: Effect Modification by Vaccination: A Case-Control Study of Comorbidities and Severe Pediatric Hospitalizations for Mycoplasma pneumoniae and SARS-CoV-2
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, pediatric hospitalizations for respiratory pathogens like *Mycoplasma pneumoniae* and SARS-CoV-2 impose a significant public health burden, with underlying comorbidities known to increase severity. However, the degree to which vaccination mitigates this elevated risk for children with specific conditions remains poorly quantified. Understanding this effect modification is critical for refining risk stratification and vaccination policy to prevent severe outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that vaccination significantly attenuates the strength of association between specific pediatric comorbidities (e.g., asthma, immunocompromised status) and the risk of severe hospitalization (ICU admission, prolonged stay) for *M. pneumoniae* and SARS-CoV-2, effectively reducing health disparities in outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective case-control study using electronic health records from US children's hospitals (2018-2025). Cases will be children hospitalized with severe disease; controls will be non-hospitalized or mildly ill outpatients. We will use multivariable logistic regression with interaction terms between vaccination status (documented receipt of COVID-19 or *M. pneumoniae* vaccine, if available pre-2026) and specific comorbidities. The primary analysis will estimate adjusted odds ratios for severe outcomes, stratifying by vaccination status to calculate measures of effect modification (e.g., relative excess risk due to interaction).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative estimates of how vaccination modifies comorbidity-associated risk for two major pediatric pathogens. It will directly inform clinical guidance on which children benefit most from vaccination and booster schedules. The findings will add a critical layer to risk prediction models by integrating vaccination as a dynamic modifier of baseline risk.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has established independent risks from comorbidities and protective effects of vaccination, this study uniquely focuses on their statistical interaction. It applies a formal effect modification framework, common in epidemiology, to the specific context of pediatric vaccine impact assessment, moving beyond estimating main effects alone.

proposals.title: A Global Consortium Study of Pediatric Pneumonia Severity: The Role of Healthcare Systems, Antibiotic Use, and Host Genetics in Hospitalized Children
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, pediatric pneumonia remains a leading cause of hospitalization and mortality globally, with significant variation in outcomes across countries. While pathogens like Mycoplasma pneumoniae are well-studied, the independent contributions of healthcare system factors (e.g., access, testing protocols, follow-up care) versus host genetic susceptibility to disease severity are poorly understood. A multi-country comparison is critical to disentangle these drivers and inform equitable, effective public health strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that national differences in pediatric pneumonia severity are driven more strongly by variations in healthcare system practices, including antibiotic stewardship and diagnostic testing, than by population genetic differences. Specifically, we posit that systems with higher rates of inappropriate antibiotic use and lower rates of targeted pathogen testing will correlate with increased disease severity, independent of genetic background.
proposals.method: We will establish a retrospective cohort of children hospitalized with community-acquired pneumonia (2018-2024) from consortium hospitals in 6-8 nations with diverse healthcare systems and genetic ancestries. We will apply a multi-level modeling framework, nesting patient-level data (demographics, genetic ancestry markers, pathogen data from PCR/urine antigen testing, antibiotic exposure) within hospital- and country-level variables (healthcare access metrics, national antibiotic prescription guidelines, standard testing protocols). The primary outcome, a composite severity score, will be analyzed using mixed-effects regression to partition variance attributable to each level.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative estimate of the relative contribution of healthcare system factors versus host genetics to severe pediatric pneumonia outcomes across diverse global settings. It will generate actionable evidence for health policymakers on which system-level interventions (e.g., standardizing diagnostic testing, improving follow-up visit protocols) may have the greatest impact on reducing severity, irrespective of genetic background.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined single pathogens or national datasets, this study uniquely integrates detailed clinical, microbiological, and healthcare system data across multiple countries within a single analytical framework to formally test the system vs. genetics hypothesis, moving beyond descriptive comparisons.

proposals.title: Protective Factors Against Hospitalization in Children with Community-Acquired Pneumonia: A Case-Control Study of Outpatient Management
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, pediatric pneumonia remains a leading cause of hospitalization, with rising concerns about antibiotic resistance and healthcare costs. While research often focuses on severe hospitalized cases, understanding factors that enable successful outpatient management is critical for developing risk-stratification tools and reducing unnecessary hospital visits and antibiotic use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific clinical, microbiological, and care-pathway factors—such as timely diagnosis with point-of-care testing, appropriate first-line antibiotic selection, and structured follow-up plans—are independently associated with a child's ability to remain out of the hospital despite a diagnosis of community-acquired pneumonia.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study using linked electronic health record and claims data from large pediatric primary care networks (2018-2024). Cases are children aged 1-17 years diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia who were managed entirely as outpatients. Controls are children with pneumonia who required hospitalization within 48 hours of diagnosis. We will use multivariable logistic regression to identify protective factors, adjusting for age, comorbidities, and pathogen (e.g., Mycoplasma pneumoniae vs. other). Key exposures include use of point-of-care tests (e.g., rapid antigen, CRP), antibiotic choice (e.g., amoxicillin vs. macrolides), and scheduling of a follow-up visit within 48-72 hours.
proposals.contribution: This study will quantify modifiable factors in the ambulatory care pathway that protect against hospitalization, providing evidence for optimized outpatient management guidelines. It will directly inform clinical decision-support tools aimed at safely reducing hospital admissions and inappropriate antibiotic use in children with pneumonia.
proposals.novelty: This work shifts the focus from risk factors for severe disease to protective factors for mild disease, leveraging detailed outpatient data often absent from hospital-centric studies. It integrates pathogen-specific data (e.g., M. pneumoniae) with real-world care delivery variables to model the probability of successful outpatient follow-through.

proposals.title: Optimizing Outpatient Parenteral Antimicrobial Therapy (OPAT) for Pediatric Pneumonia: A Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic Model to Balance Efficacy and Resistance Selection
proposals.motivation: Outpatient Parenteral Antimicrobial Therapy (OPAT) is crucial for treating serious infections like pediatric pneumonia, but prolonged antibiotic exposure can drive antimicrobial resistance (AMR). Pre-2026, OPAT regimens are often standardized, lacking patient-specific optimization to minimize the selection pressure for resistant pathogens, such as Mycoplasma pneumoniae or Streptococcus pneumoniae, while ensuring cure. This is a critical gap in pediatric infectious disease management.
