proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling framework, integrating patient-specific factors and pathogen susceptibility, can identify OPAT dosing regimens that achieve a high probability of clinical cure while minimizing the time that drug concentrations exceed the mutant selection window (MSW).
proposals.method: We will develop a mechanistic PK/PD model using pre-2026 data from pediatric OPAT programs and published parameters for common antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone). The model will simulate drug concentration-time profiles and bacterial killing dynamics. We will employ a multi-objective optimization algorithm (e.g., NSGA-II) to identify Pareto-optimal regimens that maximize the probability of target attainment (PTA) for efficacy (e.g., fT>MIC) and minimize the integrated selection pressure, defined as the area under the curve where drug concentrations fall within the pathogen's MSW. The model will be validated against retrospective clinical outcomes and resistance emergence data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, patient-centered framework for designing OPAT regimens, moving beyond one-size-fits-all approaches. It will contribute a novel, actionable metric for quantifying resistance selection risk in OPAT and offer clinicians a tool to make informed trade-offs between efficacy and AMR prevention in children.
proposals.novelty: While PK/PD models for efficacy are established, their explicit integration with the mutant selection window concept and application to multi-day OPAT scheduling in children is novel. This approach directly operationalizes the dual goal of treating infection and preventing resistance at the point of care.

proposals.title: Safety and Monitoring Outcomes for Novel Beta-Lactam/Beta-Lactamase Inhibitors in Pediatric Outpatient Parenteral Antimicrobial Therapy (OPAT): A Multi-Center Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Outpatient Parenteral Antimicrobial Therapy (OPAT) is crucial for managing serious infections in children, reducing hospital stays and healthcare costs. However, the safety and monitoring protocols for OPAT are largely derived from experience with older antibiotics. With the increasing use of novel antimicrobial agents like ceftolozane-tazobactam and ceftazidime-avibactam for multidrug-resistant infections, there is an urgent need to establish evidence-based safety frameworks specific to these agents in the pediatric OPAT setting, where monitoring resources differ from inpatient care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pediatric patients receiving novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitors via OPAT will have a distinct and quantifiable profile of adverse events and monitoring requirements compared to those receiving traditional OPAT antibiotics, and that a structured monitoring protocol can be developed to mitigate these risks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of children (<18 years) discharged on OPAT from 2019-2025. Using electronic health record data, we will identify cohorts receiving novel agents (e.g., ceftolozane-tazobactam) and matched controls receiving traditional agents (e.g., ceftriaxone). Primary outcomes will be OPAT-related adverse events (e.g., nephrotoxicity, Clostridioides difficile infection, line complications) and unplanned healthcare utilization (ED visits, readmissions). We will employ multivariable logistic regression to identify risk factors and use time-to-event analysis (Kaplan-Meier, Cox proportional hazards) to model the probability of adverse outcomes as a function of time on therapy, adapting methods from prior work on infection risk over time.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, pediatric-specific safety data for novel antibiotics in OPAT, directly informing clinical guidelines. It will yield a risk-stratified monitoring framework, specifying necessary follow-up visit frequency and laboratory testing schedules, to optimize the safe scale-up of these critical agents in ambulatory care.
proposals.novelty: While prior OPAT research focuses on established drugs, this work explicitly addresses the safety data gap for next-generation antibiotics in children, integrating pharmacovigilance with ambulatory care models and applying temporal risk analysis from infectious disease epidemiology to OPAT outcomes.

proposals.title: Early Oral Antiviral Intervention to Prevent Severe Respiratory Disease in High-Risk Children: A Pragmatic, Cluster-Randomized Trial in Ambulatory Care
proposals.motivation: Current clinical guidelines for respiratory viruses like influenza and SARS-CoV-2 often reserve antivirals for severe or hospitalized cases, a reactive strategy that may miss a critical window to alter disease course. Children, especially those with comorbidities, are a high-risk group for progression from mild to severe disease, leading to increased healthcare visits and hospitalizations. Shifting to a proactive, test-and-treat model in ambulatory settings could reduce severe outcomes and healthcare burden.
proposals.hypothesis: In children at high risk for severe respiratory illness, early identification of viral infection and immediate initiation of oral antiviral therapy during a mild, ambulatory presentation will significantly reduce the rate of progression to severe disease requiring hospitalization within 14 days, compared to standard supportive care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial at primary care pediatric clinics. Children (1-17 years) with mild viral symptoms and high-risk conditions (e.g., asthma, immunocompromise) will be tested at the point-of-care for a panel of respiratory viruses (influenza, SARS-CoV-2, RSV). Clinics will be randomized to an intervention arm (immediate prescription of appropriate oral antiviral, e.g., oseltamivir or nirmatrelvir/ritonavir, based on test result) or a control arm (standard care with antivirals per current guidelines). The primary outcome is hospitalization for respiratory complications within 14 days. We will use a mixed-effects logistic regression model, accounting for clinic-level clustering and adjusting for age, specific virus, and comorbidity, to analyze the effect of the intervention.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-quality evidence to inform new clinical guidelines for antiviral use in pediatric ambulatory care. It will quantify the potential benefit of a pre-emptive treatment strategy in a vulnerable population. The findings could directly translate into a scalable intervention to decrease pediatric hospitalizations during seasonal respiratory virus outbreaks.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the established paradigm of antiviral use from a rescue therapy for severe disease to a preventive intervention for mild, high-risk cases, specifically in children. It leverages recent advances in rapid, multiplex point-of-care testing and applies a pragmatic trial design directly within the workflow of community pediatric care.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Platform Trial for Evaluating Oral Antiviral Regimens in Children Hospitalized with Mycoplasma pneumoniae
proposals.motivation: Pediatric trials for antivirals face significant recruitment challenges, leading to delays in evidence generation and off-label use. This is critical for pathogens like Mycoplasma pneumoniae, which causes substantial pediatric hospitalizations and lacks optimal, approved therapies. Efficient trial designs are urgently needed to evaluate multiple candidate regimens within constrained pediatric populations.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian platform trial design, incorporating response-adaptive randomization and shared control arms, will efficiently identify superior antiviral regimens for pediatric M. pneumoniae infection compared to standard sequential randomized controlled trials.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-arm, multi-stage platform trial for children (2-18 years) hospitalized with M. pneumoniae. The design will use a Bayesian hierarchical model to share information across treatment arms and a common placebo/standard-of-care control. Response-adaptive randomization (e.g., Thompson sampling) will allocate more participants to promising regimens based on a primary composite endpoint of time to clinical recovery and PCR clearance from oropharyngeal samples. Futility and superiority will be assessed via pre-specified posterior probability thresholds. The platform will allow for the addition of new antiviral arms during the trial.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, statistical framework for conducting efficient pediatric antiviral trials, directly addressing sample size limitations. It will generate comparative effectiveness data for multiple M. pneumoniae antiviral candidates, potentially accelerating regulatory pathways. The methodology will be a proof-of-concept for adapting complex oncology trial designs to pediatric infectious diseases.
proposals.novelty: While platform trials are established in oncology, their application to pediatric antimicrobials is nascent. This proposal uniquely integrates adaptive designs from oncology with precise microbiological endpoints (inspired by recent M. pneumoniae and viral testing work) for a common, yet therapeutically ambiguous, pediatric respiratory pathogen.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Oral Antiviral Agents for Influenza in Children: A Multi-Center, Real-World Evidence Study Using Electronic Health Records
proposals.motivation: Influenza remains a leading cause of pediatric hospitalization and outpatient visits. While oral antivirals like oseltamivir are available, real-world evidence on their comparative effectiveness in diverse pediatric populations across different care settings is limited. Pre-2026, the expansion of large-scale EHR networks and pharmacoepidemiologic methods presents an opportunity to rigorously evaluate antiviral outcomes in children, informing clinical and public health guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that early initiation (≤48 hours of symptom onset) of oral antiviral therapy is associated with a reduced risk of subsequent healthcare encounters (hospitalization, urgent care, follow-up visits) within 14 days for children diagnosed with influenza, and that this effect varies by age, comorbid conditions, and antiviral type.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using de-identified EHR data from a national network of pediatric hospitals and affiliated primary care clinics (e.g., PEDSnet, 2015-2024). Children aged 1-17 years with an influenza diagnosis (ICD-10 codes) and an oral antiviral prescription will be included. We will apply a target trial emulation framework, using propensity score matching to balance confounders between early and late/no treatment groups. The primary outcome is a composite of hospital admission or urgent outpatient revisit. We will use Cox proportional hazards models to estimate hazard ratios, adjusting for clustered data by site.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide large-scale, real-world evidence on the effectiveness of influenza antivirals in children, directly applicable to ambulatory and hospital practice. It will quantify the impact of treatment timing and identify subpopulations with the greatest potential benefit, supporting more targeted therapeutic recommendations. The analysis will also demonstrate the application of advanced pharmacoepidemiologic methods to pediatric antiviral research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on antiviral efficacy in controlled trials or single centers, this study leverages multi-center EHR data to emulate a pragmatic trial across the continuum of care, integrating the researcher's expertise in pediatric infections (M. pneumoniae, viral testing) and longitudinal outcome analysis (probability of infection over time).

proposals.title: Immunological and Genetic Correlates of Asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 Infection in Children: A Case-Control Study
proposals.motivation: While children often experience less severe COVID-19, the mechanisms underlying the high proportion of asymptomatic infections remain poorly defined. Identifying the factors that confer natural protection could reveal novel therapeutic targets and inform risk stratification, complementing existing research on disease susceptibility and progression. This 'inverse problem' approach, focusing on resilience, is understudied relative to research on severe disease.
proposals.hypothesis: Children who remain asymptomatic following confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection possess distinct immunological signatures (e.g., pre-existing cross-reactive T-cells, mucosal IgA) and/or genetic variants (e.g., in innate immune sensors like TLRs or MHC class I genes) compared to children who develop symptomatic infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study leveraging pre-2025 pediatric cohorts with serial SARS-CoV-2 PCR testing, such as household transmission studies, to identify asymptomatic (cases) and symptomatic (controls) infected children. Using stored biospecimens, we will perform high-dimensional immune profiling (Olink or Luminex panels for cytokines/chemokines, flow cytometry for T-cell subsets) and targeted sequencing of candidate immune genes (e.g., using Illumina NextSeq). We will apply multivariate logistic regression and machine learning (e.g., Random Forest or LASSO regression) to identify predictive signatures of asymptomatic status, controlling for covariates like age and viral load.
proposals.contribution: This study will define specific immune and genetic factors associated with asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection in children, moving beyond epidemiological observation to mechanistic insight. The findings could identify biomarkers of resilience and inform the development of broadly protective mucosal vaccines or immunomodulatory therapies.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the typical severe disease framework to focus on natural protection, directly integrating immune and genetic profiling in a pediatric-specific design. It applies an 'inverse problem' methodology common in engineering to a biological question of clinical importance, which is distinct from prior work on infection probability or bacterial/viral co-infections.

proposals.title: Development and Feasibility of a Home-Based Symptom and Pulse Oximetry Monitoring Protocol to Prevent Severe Disease in High-Risk Children with Mild COVID-19
proposals.motivation: During the COVID-19 pandemic, children with underlying conditions (e.g., chronic lung disease, immunocompromise) were at higher risk for severe outcomes. Hospital systems were often strained, and managing mild cases at home was a necessity, yet protocols for identifying early deterioration in high-risk pediatric populations were lacking. This creates an urgent need for evidence-based, scalable remote monitoring to ensure safety and conserve healthcare resources.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, home-based monitoring protocol using caregiver-reported symptoms and twice-daily pulse oximetry will enable the early detection of clinical deterioration in high-risk children with mild COVID-19, facilitating timely medical intervention and reducing the need for preventable emergency department visits or hospitalizations.
proposals.method: We will develop a protocol defining 'high-risk' conditions (e.g., asthma, complex chronic conditions) and 'mild' COVID-19. Caregivers will be provided with Bluetooth-enabled pulse oximeters and a mobile app for daily symptom logging (e.g., respiratory rate, activity tolerance, fever). Data will be transmitted to a central dashboard monitored by study nurses. We will implement a decision algorithm, based on pre-2026 pediatric early warning scores and COVID-19 progression literature, to trigger nurse-led telephone follow-up or escalation to a physician visit based on specific oximetry thresholds (e.g., SpO2 ≤94%) or symptom clusters. Feasibility will be assessed via recruitment rates, protocol adherence, and time-to-intervention.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, practical monitoring protocol for a vulnerable pediatric population, directly applicable to outpatient and telehealth practice. It will provide quantitative evidence on the feasibility and potential effectiveness of home monitoring to prevent severe disease progression, contributing to pandemic preparedness and the management of other respiratory illnesses in high-risk children.
proposals.novelty: While remote monitoring for adults with COVID-19 was explored pre-2026, pediatric-specific protocols, particularly those integrating simple biometrics with caregiver-reported outcomes for high-risk children in a home setting, are novel. This work directly extends the researcher's expertise in pediatric infection follow-up and testing into the domain of decentralized care.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity in Pediatric Antibiotic Use: A Multi-Setting Analysis of Visit Severity and Diagnostic Testing for Respiratory Illnesses
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on antibiotic prescribing often aggregates data across ambulatory settings, potentially masking critical differences in patient populations and clinical decision-making. This conflation limits our understanding of drivers of inappropriate antibiotic use, a key factor in antimicrobial resistance. Differentiating visits by setting (e.g., primary care, urgent care, emergency departments) is essential to tailor stewardship interventions effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pediatric visits for respiratory symptoms across different ambulatory settings represent distinct populations in terms of illness severity and diagnostic uncertainty, leading to setting-specific patterns of diagnostic testing (e.g., for Mycoplasma pneumoniae) and antibiotic prescribing that are not explained by diagnosis alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using national ambulatory care databases (e.g., NAMCS, NHAMCS) from 2016-2023. We will identify pediatric visits for acute respiratory infections. Using multilevel logistic regression, we will model the probability of antibiotic prescription and specific diagnostic testing (e.g., urine testing, respiratory pathogen panels) as a function of visit setting, controlling for diagnosis, patient demographics, and comorbidities. We will explicitly test for interaction effects between setting and clinical factors to identify setting-specific decision pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a nuanced, setting-stratified map of pediatric antibiotic use and diagnostic testing patterns for respiratory illnesses. It will identify which settings have the highest discordance between testing results and treatment, offering actionable targets for precision stewardship. The findings will advance methods for analyzing healthcare utilization data by treating setting as a key effect modifier rather than a confounder.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard epidemiological assumption that a 'visit' is a comparable unit of analysis across settings. Instead, we treat the care setting as a proxy for a latent variable encompassing disease severity, diagnostic urgency, and clinician risk tolerance, which fundamentally shapes management decisions.

proposals.title: An Agent-Based Model of Pediatric Antibiotic Prescribing: Simulating the Impact of Diagnostic Testing and Follow-Up on Ambulatory Care Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Inappropriate antibiotic prescribing for pediatric respiratory infections, often driven by diagnostic uncertainty and patient/parent expectations, remains a key driver of antimicrobial resistance. Pre-2026, the interplay between point-of-care testing (e.g., for Mycoplasma pneumoniae or viruses), clinician decision heuristics, and the likelihood of follow-up visits is poorly quantified, limiting the design of effective stewardship interventions in ambulatory settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an agent-based model (ABM) incorporating realistic patient-caregiver and prescriber agents will reveal that increased use of specific diagnostic testing (e.g., for M. pneumoniae) and structured follow-up protocols can significantly reduce unnecessary antibiotic prescriptions without increasing return visits for clinical worsening.
proposals.method: We will develop an ABM using a framework like NetLogo or Mesa. The model will simulate a population of caregiver-child agents (with attributes like age, symptom severity, and care-seeking propensity) and clinician agents (with attributes like testing preference, risk aversion, and prescribing threshold). Agent decision rules will be informed by pre-2026 literature and calibrated using data from Dr. Hersh's prior work on M. pneumoniae hospitalizations and national ambulatory visit patterns. The simulation will test scenarios varying testing availability and mandated follow-up rules, with outcomes measured in prescription rates and visit counts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic simulation tool to explore 'what-if' scenarios for outpatient stewardship. It will quantify the potential impact of diagnostic and follow-up strategies on prescribing dynamics, offering actionable insights for healthcare systems aiming to reduce antibiotic overuse in children.
proposals.novelty: While ABMs are used in epidemiology, their application to model the micro-level decision-making interactions between parents and prescribers in pediatric ambulatory care, explicitly integrating diagnostic test results and follow-up visit logistics, is a novel synthesis of social science methodology and clinical epidemiology.

proposals.title: Individual Clinician-Level Variation in Antibiotic Prescribing for Pediatric Acute Respiratory Visits: A National Analysis of Follow-Up Patterns and Mycoplasma pneumoniae Testing
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic stewardship efforts in pediatric ambulatory care have traditionally focused on institutional or setting-level metrics, masking significant variation among individual prescribers. This variation is critical, as individual clinician decisions directly influence patient outcomes, follow-up visit burden, and the development of antimicrobial resistance. Understanding prescriber-level patterns, particularly in the context of specific pathogens like Mycoplasma pneumoniae and the use of diagnostic testing, is a necessary next step for targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Within the same care setting (e.g., pediatric emergency departments, primary care offices), individual clinicians exhibit statistically significant and clinically meaningful variation in antibiotic prescribing rates for acute respiratory infections, which is associated with differential use of M. pneumoniae testing and rates of scheduled follow-up visits.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using a national administrative claims database (e.g., IBM MarketScan, 2018-2024). The unit of analysis will be the individual clinician (de-identified via NPI). For each clinician, we will calculate risk-standardized antibiotic prescribing rates for pediatric acute respiratory visits. We will employ hierarchical logistic regression models, nesting visits within clinicians and clinicians within facilities, to isolate clinician-level effects. The models will assess the association between prescribing rates and two key outcomes: the rate of M. pneumoniae testing (urine antigen or PCR) ordered during the index visit and the probability of a related follow-up visit within 14 days, adapting time-to-event methods from prior SARS-CoV-2 infection probability work.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first national, quantitative profile of individual clinician prescribing variation in pediatric respiratory care, directly linking it to testing practices and healthcare utilization. It will identify a high-priority subset of clinicians for stewardship outreach and generate evidence on whether low-value testing (e.g., routine viral URI urine testing) is concentrated among high prescribers.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Hersh et al. analyzed testing and visits at the setting level, this proposal shifts the analytical lens to the individual prescriber, a more actionable unit for intervention. It uniquely integrates analyses of prescribing, pathogen-specific testing, and follow-up visits at this granular level to create a composite picture of clinical decision-making.

proposals.title: Determinants of Guideline-Adherent Antibiotic Prescribing for Pediatric Pneumonia: A Multi-Center Case-Control Study of Ambulatory Visits
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic stewardship efforts often focus on reducing inappropriate prescribing, but understanding the drivers of *appropriate* prescribing is equally critical for designing effective interventions. In pediatric ambulatory care, where community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) is a leading cause of antibiotic use, guideline-concordant therapy remains suboptimal, potentially impacting child health outcomes and resistance patterns. This study inverts the typical focus to model the factors that successfully lead to correct, necessary antibiotic decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that appropriate antibiotic prescribing for pediatric CAP is independently associated with specific clinical (e.g., presence of focal lung findings), diagnostic (e.g., point-of-care testing for Mycoplasma pneumoniae), and systemic (e.g., stewardship program elements) factors identifiable in electronic health record data.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study using EHR data from a network of pediatric hospitals and affiliated care clinics (2018-2024). Cases are children (2-17 years) diagnosed with CAP who received first-line, guideline-adherent antibiotic therapy (amoxicillin or amoxicillin-clavulanate for typical CAP, macrolides for suspected M. pneumoniae). Controls are children with CAP who received non-adherent therapy. We will use multivariable logistic regression to identify predictors of appropriate prescribing, adjusting for demographics, visit setting, and comorbidities. A secondary analysis will apply causal forest algorithms to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects of diagnostic testing (e.g., urine antigen testing for pneumococcus) on prescribing appropriateness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a positive model for stewardship by identifying actionable, real-world correlates of correct prescribing, moving beyond deficit-based analyses. It will offer evidence for which diagnostic strategies and clinical practices most reliably support guideline-concordant care. The findings will directly inform the design of stewardship tools and clinical decision support aimed at reinforcing effective behaviors.
proposals.novelty: While most stewardship research quantifies inappropriate use, this study explicitly models and identifies the determinants of *appropriate* use as a template for improvement. It integrates the researcher's expertise in M. pneumoniae epidemiology, diagnostic testing utilization, and temporal analysis of infection risk into a novel, positive-framework analysis.

proposals.title: Clinic-Level Drivers of Antibiotic Prescribing for Pediatric Respiratory Illness: A Mixed-Methods Case Study of High- and Low-Prescribing Primary Care Practices
proposals.motivation: Despite national efforts to combat antibiotic resistance, inappropriate prescribing for pediatric respiratory conditions remains high, particularly for viral illnesses where antibiotics are ineffective. Prior research, including Hersh's work on urine testing and Mycoplasma pneumoniae, often uses large administrative datasets that identify population-level trends but mask critical, modifiable factors at the clinic level where prescribing decisions occur. A granular, clinic-focused analysis is needed to understand the local clinical culture, diagnostic practices, and patient flow that drive prescribing variation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that clinics with low antibiotic prescribing rates for pediatric acute respiratory tract infections (ARTIs) will demonstrate systematically different clinical workflows, diagnostic test utilization (e.g., point-of-care testing for influenza, Mycoplasma pneumoniae, or streptococcal pharyngitis), and patterns of follow-up visits compared to high-prescribing clinics, independent of patient case-mix.
proposals.method: We will conduct a convergent mixed-methods case study of 4-6 pediatric primary care clinics, selected from a national network based on extreme, risk-adjusted antibiotic prescribing rates for ARTIs (2019-2024). Quantitative analysis will use de-identified electronic health record data to model visit-level prescribing probability, employing multivariable logistic regression with clinic as a fixed effect, controlling for demographics and coded diagnoses. Concurrently, we will perform ethnographic observations and structured interviews with clinicians and staff to map decision-making workflows, test-ordering habits, and communication strategies regarding antibiotic use and follow-up plans. Quantitative and qualitative data will be integrated to build explanatory models for each clinic.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond identifying 'what' the prescribing variation is to explain 'how' and 'why' it persists at the point of care. It will generate a detailed taxonomy of clinic-level factors—from test availability to scheduling protocols—that enable or deter appropriate prescribing. The findings will provide actionable, context-specific templates for designing and implementing antimicrobial stewardship interventions directly within the clinic environment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Hersh and others quantified testing and prescribing at regional or national levels, this proposal's novelty lies in its intensive, ground-level focus on individual clinics as the unit of analysis, combining advanced visit-level probability modeling with real-time clinical ethnography to uncover the mechanistic drivers of prescribing behavior.

proposals.title: Trajectories of Care Following Index Pneumonia Hospitalization in Chronically Ill Children: A Retrospective Cohort Study of Antibiotic Use and Healthcare Re-utilization
proposals.motivation: Pneumonia is a leading cause of pediatric hospitalization, but current clinical and research paradigms often treat it as an isolated event. For children with chronic conditions, this index hospitalization may be a sentinel event within a longer, more complex disease trajectory. Understanding the post-discharge course is critical to optimizing antibiotic stewardship and preventing subsequent severe illness, especially given rising concerns about antimicrobial resistance and pathogens like Mycoplasma pneumoniae.
proposals.hypothesis: In children with underlying chronic conditions, an index hospitalization for community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) is not an isolated event but is associated with a distinct, elevated trajectory of outpatient antibiotic prescriptions and healthcare visits in the subsequent 12 months, compared to matched controls without a pneumonia hospitalization.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using a national administrative claims database (e.g., IBM MarketScan, 2015-2023). Children (1-17 years) with chronic conditions and an index CAP hospitalization will be identified. A time-to-event analysis framework, building on methods from prior work on infection probability, will be used. The primary outcomes are rates of ambulatory visits (all-cause, respiratory-related) and oral antibiotic prescription fills in the 12 months post-discharge. We will use propensity score matching to create a control cohort of chronically ill children without a CAP admission and employ multivariable Poisson regression and Kaplan-Meier analyses to compare outcomes, adjusting for demographics and comorbidity burden.
proposals.contribution: This study will quantify the longitudinal burden of illness following pediatric pneumonia hospitalization, moving beyond the index event. It will provide evidence for whether these hospitalizations should trigger enhanced post-discharge monitoring and antimicrobial stewardship programs tailored to high-risk children. The findings will inform clinical guidelines on follow-up care for this vulnerable population.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has analyzed pneumonia etiology (e.g., M. pneumoniae) or diagnostic testing patterns at single visits, this study explicitly models the hospitalization as the start of a continuous care trajectory. It integrates the researcher's expertise in longitudinal infection analysis and pediatric ambulatory care to challenge the discrete-event model of pneumonia management.

proposals.title: Independent and Additive Effects of Patient Comorbidities and Hospital-Level Antibiotic Stewardship on Clinical Outcomes in Pediatric Mycoplasma pneumoniae Hospitalizations
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, pediatric Mycoplasma pneumoniae infections are a leading cause of community-acquired pneumonia, with rising macrolide resistance. While patient factors (e.g., asthma) influence severity, hospital-level practices, particularly antibiotic prescribing and diagnostic testing, vary widely. It is unknown if these factors operate independently or interact to determine outcomes like length of stay and follow-up visits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the burden of patient comorbidities and the intensity of a hospital's antibiotic stewardship practices (e.g., adherence to narrow-spectrum therapy guidelines) are independent and additive predictors of clinical outcomes in children hospitalized with M. pneumoniae.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center retrospective cohort study using pediatric administrative data (e.g., PHIS) from 2018-2024. The primary outcome is a composite of prolonged hospitalization (>75th percentile) or unplanned 30-day follow-up. We will construct two scores: a patient-level comorbidity index and a hospital-level stewardship intensity score based on guideline-concordant antibiotic use and urine/respiratory pathogen testing rates. The independent and additive effects will be tested using multivariable hierarchical logistic regression, with hospital as a random effect, and by assessing the significance of interaction terms between the two scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will quantify the separate contributions of patient vulnerability and hospital care quality to pediatric pneumonia outcomes. It will provide actionable evidence for hospitals by identifying modifiable stewardship practices that improve outcomes regardless of patient mix, moving beyond blaming comorbidities for poor results.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has analyzed patient factors or stewardship separately, this study explicitly tests their statistical independence and additivity using a two-dimensional scoring system within a causal inference framework for a specific, high-burden pathogen.

proposals.title: Dynamic Post-Discharge Medication Adherence and Risk of Rehospitalization in Children Treated for Community-Acquired Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Hospital readmissions for pediatric pneumonia are a significant burden, but risk prediction models rely heavily on static, pre-discharge factors (e.g., age, comorbidities). This ignores potentially modifiable post-discharge behaviors, particularly adherence to prescribed oral antibiotics, which is critical for resolving infections like Mycoplasma pneumoniae. Understanding the dynamic relationship between adherence trajectories and readmission risk is essential for designing effective post-discharge interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamic patterns of antibiotic adherence in the 30 days post-discharge, measured via pharmacy refill data and caregiver-reported surveys, are significant independent predictors of 90-day all-cause readmission, even after adjusting for static clinical and demographic risk factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center retrospective cohort study of children (1-18 years) hospitalized for community-acquired pneumonia (2018-2024) with discharge prescriptions for oral antibiotics. The primary exposure is a time-varying measure of medication adherence, calculated using the proportion of days covered (PDC) from pharmacy claims, updated weekly post-discharge. We will use a Cox proportional hazards model with time-dependent covariates to model the hazard of readmission as a function of this dynamic adherence measure, static covariates (from prior work: age, severity, pathogen), and healthcare utilization (from ambulatory visit data). Sensitivity analyses will use joint longitudinal-survival models to account for measurement error in adherence.
proposals.contribution: This study will quantify the independent contribution of a dynamic, post-discharge factor to readmission risk, moving beyond static prediction. It will provide evidence for targeting adherence support in post-discharge care plans. The methodological approach of integrating time-dependent behavioral data into readmission risk models can be applied to other pediatric conditions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has linked poor adherence to outcomes, this study uniquely models adherence as a time-varying exposure in a survival framework specific to pediatric pneumonia readmission, directly testing the modifiability of a post-discharge risk factor against established static predictors.

proposals.title: Impact of Structured Discharge Processes and Timely Follow-up on 30-Day Readmissions in Children Hospitalized with Mycoplasma pneumoniae and Other Respiratory Infections
proposals.motivation: Hospital readmissions for pediatric respiratory infections, including Mycoplasma pneumoniae, represent a significant burden on patients, families, and healthcare systems. Pre-2026 evidence highlights high rates of M. pneumoniae in hospitalized children and frequent but potentially unnecessary testing in ambulatory settings, yet the role of post-discharge care processes in preventing readmissions is poorly characterized. Optimizing discharge and follow-up is critical for improving outcomes and resource use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured discharge bundle (including specific antibiotic guidance, symptom action plans, and scheduled follow-up) and completion of an outpatient follow-up visit within 7 days of discharge are associated with a significantly lower risk of 30-day all-cause readmission among children hospitalized for respiratory infections.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from 2018-2024 for children aged 1-18 years hospitalized with M. pneumoniae or other community-acquired pneumonia. The primary exposure will be documentation of a structured discharge process. We will use multivariable logistic regression, adjusting for age, comorbidities, and illness severity, to model the odds of readmission. A time-to-event analysis (Cox proportional hazards model) will assess the impact of timing of the first follow-up visit.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide actionable evidence on modifiable discharge and follow-up factors to reduce pediatric readmissions. It will extend Dr. Hersh's work on M. pneumoniae epidemiology and ambulatory testing by linking inpatient management to post-discharge outcomes, directly informing clinical practice and quality improvement initiatives.
proposals.novelty: While readmission predictors are studied, this work uniquely integrates specific discharge process elements with objective follow-up timing data to quantify their combined effect, moving beyond demographic or clinical risk factors to focus on actionable care transitions.

proposals.title: Identifying Protective Discharge and Follow-up Practices in Pediatric Pneumonia: An Inverse-Variance Weighted Analysis of Low-Readmission Hospitals
proposals.motivation: Hospital readmissions for pediatric pneumonia represent a significant burden on patients, families, and healthcare systems, indicating potential gaps in discharge planning and outpatient follow-up. Prior research, including Hersh's work on *Mycoplasma pneumoniae* hospitalizations, has characterized risk factors for severe disease, but less is known about modifiable hospital-level practices that prevent readmission. An inverse-problem approach, focusing on high-performing outliers, can efficiently identify protective strategies for implementation.
proposals.hypothesis: Hospitals with pediatric pneumonia readmission rates significantly lower than predicted by patient case-mix and severity will demonstrate distinct, identifiable patterns in discharge processes, antibiotic stewardship, and structured post-discharge follow-up.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-state retrospective cohort study using pediatric (age <18 years) hospital discharge data (2019-2024) linked to ambulatory claims. First, we will fit a hierarchical logistic regression model to predict 30-day all-cause readmission risk based on demographics, comorbidities (e.g., asthma), and severity (e.g., ICU admission). Hospitals will be ranked by their inverse-variance weighted risk-standardized readmission ratio. We will then perform a mixed-methods case study of the top decile of 'low-readmission' hospitals, quantitatively analyzing their documented discharge practices (e.g., antibiotic duration guidance, scheduled follow-up visit timing) and qualitatively interviewing clinical leads to identify protective protocols.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond identifying risk factors to empirically derive a set of actionable, hospital-level protective practices associated with reduced readmissions for pediatric pneumonia. It will provide a methodological framework for applying inverse-problem analysis to other pediatric conditions and generate specific evidence to inform clinical guidelines on discharge planning and follow-up care coordination.
proposals.novelty: This approach inverts the typical risk-factor analysis to learn from success rather than failure, and uniquely integrates Hersh's expertise in pediatric pneumonia epidemiology, antibiotic use, and ambulatory visit patterns to investigate the critical transition from inpatient to outpatient care.

proposals.title: A Retrospective Cohort Analysis of 30-Day Readmission Risk Factors for Pediatric Asthma, Bronchiolitis, and Dehydration in U.S. Emergency Departments and Ambulatory Care Settings
proposals.motivation: Asthma, bronchiolitis, and dehydration are leading causes of pediatric acute care visits and hospitalizations. Unplanned readmissions following initial management represent a significant burden on families and healthcare systems, yet comprehensive, condition-specific analyses of readmission risk factors in diverse ambulatory settings are limited. Applying a rigorous readmission framework to these high-volume conditions can identify modifiable factors to improve care transitions and reduce preventable return visits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific demographic, clinical (e.g., severity markers, co-infections), and care process factors (e.g., antibiotic prescribing, follow-up timing) identifiable during the index visit are independently associated with increased 30-day readmission risk for pediatric asthma, bronchiolitis, and dehydration.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using a national administrative claims database (e.g., IBM MarketScan, 2015-2023). Children (<18 years) with index ED or outpatient visits for asthma, bronchiolitis, or dehydration will be identified. The primary outcome is an unplanned all-cause readmission within 30 days. We will apply the statistical framework from Hersh's prior SARS-CoV-2 susceptibility work, using multivariable Cox proportional hazards models to calculate hazard ratios for candidate risk factors (e.g., age, comorbidities, diagnostic testing, antibiotic/steroid use). Subgroup and sensitivity analyses will be performed.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, comparative analysis of readmission drivers across three major pediatric conditions using a unified methodological framework. It will generate condition-specific risk profiles to inform targeted interventions and clinical decision support. The findings will extend the evidence base beyond infectious diseases like *Mycoplasma pneumoniae* and urinary tract infections to other high-volume ambulatory care-sensitive conditions.
proposals.novelty: While readmission studies exist, few simultaneously analyze and compare multiple non-infectious and infectious high-volume pediatric conditions using a sophisticated time-to-event framework previously applied to infection risk, creating a novel cross-condition perspective on a common healthcare quality metric.

ideas.title: Causal inference to quantify misclassification bias and treatment effects in children discharged from the ED
ideas.description: This research direction uses causal inference methods (e.g., instrumental variables, g-methods) on observational pediatric ED data to formally test the assumption that discharge equals mild illness. By treating this assumption as a potential source of misclassification bias, the analysis can identify a subgroup of discharged children with higher latent severity. Within this refined cohort, the methods are then applied to produce less biased estimates of antibiotic treatment effects on outcomes like return visits or hospitalization, which are often obscured when high- and low-severity discharged patients are incorrectly pooled.

ideas.description: This study embeds a dual-objective optimization algorithm within a pragmatic, cluster-randomized controlled trial. Hospital sites are randomized to either implement the algorithm, which recommends antibiotic therapy by dynamically balancing estimated cost and local resistance patterns, or to follow a standard protocol of pre-specified narrow vs. broad-spectrum therapy. The primary outcome is a composite of clinical failure and cost, while secondary outcomes include ecological measures of antimicrobial resistance and algorithm adherence. This design simultaneously tests the superiority of a dynamic optimization approach and generates the high-quality evidence needed to calibrate and validate the model for real-world implementation.

ideas.title: Dynamic, adaptive risk monitoring for pediatric antimicrobial therapy failure
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid machine learning system for dynamic risk stratification of children on narrow-spectrum therapy. It combines a baseline model, trained on historical patient data, with a real-time adaptive component that continuously ingests and analyzes streaming clinical data during treatment. This design allows the system to detect early signs of clinical deterioration or poor response, updating the patient's risk category accordingly. The output is a continuously updated risk trajectory, enabling clinicians to re-evaluate therapy choices based on the patient's evolving condition, not just their initial presentation.

ideas.title: A Pragmatic, Cluster-Randomized Crossover Trial of Diagnostic-Guided Narrow-Spectrum Initiation vs. Usual Care: Causal Effects on Long-Term Patient Outcomes and Resistome Evolution
ideas.description: This study uses a cluster-randomized, crossover design at the hospital or clinical team level to eliminate confounding by indication when comparing antibiotic strategies. Clusters are assigned to either a rapid diagnostic-guided protocol for narrow-spectrum initiation or to continue usual broad-spectrum empiric therapy, with crossover periods to ensure balance. The primary outcome is a composite of long-term clinical failure, recurrence, and mortality over one year, while secondary outcomes use serial metagenomic sequencing to quantify the causal impact of the assigned strategy on individual patient and unit-level antimicrobial resistance gene abundance and diversity over time.

ideas.title: Ecological Succession Modeling of Patient Microbiomes to Predict Resistance Emergence
ideas.description: This research adapts ecological succession modeling from conservation biology to study long-term clinical outcomes and antimicrobial resistance. The analogous solution involves using longitudinal studies and computational models to predict how ecosystems evolve after a disturbance, focusing on community structure and invasive species emergence. The adapted idea treats antibiotic treatment as an ecological disturbance to the patient's microbiome. It proposes a long-term, longitudinal cohort study that uses advanced sequencing and ecological modeling tools to track microbial community 'succession' for years after initial antibiotic therapy. By modeling the microbiome as an ecosystem, the study aims to predict how the initial choice of narrow versus broad-spectrum antibiotics influences the long-term trajectory of the community, specifically the emergence and persistence of resistant bacterial strains ('invasive species') and the associated clinical outcomes for patients.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Theory to Adult CAP Treatment Equivalence
ideas.description: This research seed uses an analogy from community ecology to investigate the equivalence between narrow and broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy for adult community-acquired pneumonia (CAP). The analogous problem in ecosystem management—choosing between targeted species reintroduction versus broad rewilding—was solved by mapping the functional niche space of the ecosystem to predict intervention outcomes. Adapted to clinical research, the idea is to first define the multidimensional 'clinical niche' of adult CAP patients using variables such as age, comorbidities (e.g., COPD, diabetes), severity scores, local antimicrobial resistance prevalence, and host immune response biomarkers. A large-scale, observational or pragmatic trial would then test whether therapeutic equivalence is not universal but is instead contingent on a patient's position within this defined niche space. The solution involves developing a predictive model to identify the subpopulations (specific niches) where narrow-spectrum therapy (e.g., amoxicillin) is truly non-inferior to broad-spectrum therapy, thereby enabling more precise and sustainable antibiotic stewardship upon scaling to the heterogeneous adult population.

ideas.title: Adaptive Control in Antibiotic Therapy: A Signal Processing Approach to De-escalation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from adaptive filtering in signal processing, where a system starts with a broad filter and uses feedback to converge on a precise solution, this research proposes an adaptive control model for antibiotic de-escalation in children. The analogous solution involves using a real-time error signal to iteratively refine a filter's parameters. Adapted to the clinical context, the study would investigate outcomes when de-escalation from broad- to narrow-spectrum therapy is dynamically guided by a predefined algorithm processing continuous feedback from clinical response, biomarker trends (e.g., daily procalcitonin), and rapid diagnostic results, rather than a static time point. The goal is to determine if this adaptive, signal-informed de-escalation strategy improves safety, efficacy, and antimicrobial stewardship compared to standard protocols.

ideas.title: An Operational Framework for Quantifying the Prevalence and Drivers of Non-Clinical Hospitalizations
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a standardized framework to identify and quantify hospital admissions primarily driven by social or isolation needs rather than acute medical necessity. The system integrates structured data (e.g., length of stay, therapeutic interventions, bed census) with unstructured clinical note analysis to flag cases with high probability of non-clinical drivers. By applying this framework to a large, multi-hospital cohort, it will generate an evidence-based estimate of prevalence, associated costs, and patient demographics. The final output is a tool to inform hospital policy and identify systemic gaps in community-based care that contribute to potentially avoidable admissions.

ideas.title: A Real-Time Learning Health System to Detect and Characterize Post-Acute Sequelae of COVID-19 in Pediatric Populations
ideas.description: This research implements a learning health system framework using linked, longitudinal electronic health record data to monitor pediatric populations in real-time. The system is specifically designed to identify children with no prior chronic conditions who develop new diagnoses (e.g., diabetes, mood disorders, fatigue syndromes) following acute COVID-19 infection, thereby testing the inverted comorbidity hypothesis. The continuous data stream allows for the detection of emerging patterns and temporal clusters of post-acute sequelae, enabling rapid hypothesis generation. Critically, the system feeds these findings back to refine clinical decision support and monitoring guidelines, creating an adaptive loop that improves detection and management as new knowledge about pediatric post-COVID conditions is generated.

ideas.title: Pareto-Optimal Antibiotic Selection via Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithms
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from evolutionary robotics and engineering design to the medical context of pneumonia therapy. The analogous problem—optimizing for multiple, conflicting objectives like weight and strength in component design—is solved using Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithms (MOEAs like NSGA-II) to find the Pareto-optimal frontier. We adapt this by applying an MOEA to model the dual objectives of minimizing direct treatment cost and minimizing the predicted contribution to antimicrobial resistance (AMR) when selecting antibiotic therapy for pneumonia. The algorithm would process patient and pathogen-specific data (e.g., local resistance patterns, drug prices) to evolve and present a set of non-dominated optimal treatment strategies on a cost-AMR trade-off frontier. This provides a decision-support tool where the optimal balance between immediate cost and long-term resistance can be visually analyzed and selected based on dynamic institutional or public health priorities.

ideas.title: Emulating a Target Trial to Estimate the Causal Effect of Vaccination on Comorbidity-Specific Hospitalization Risk
ideas.description: This study uses a target trial emulation framework to estimate the causal effect of vaccination on pediatric COVID-19 hospitalization risk, specifically within comorbidity subgroups. A cohort is constructed from linked healthcare data, defining an eligibility date and emulating randomization by matching children with specific comorbidities on their immediate propensity to be vaccinated at that time. The analysis employs a multi-state model to account for competing risks and time-varying exposures, providing robust estimates of vaccine effectiveness as an effect measure modifier for each comorbidity's associated risk.

ideas.title: Deconstructing Antibiotic Categories via Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic Phenotyping
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that ampicillin/penicillin and ceftriaxone/cefotaxine are monolithic narrow and broad categories by drawing an analogy from plant ecology. In that field, rigid taxonomic groupings were superseded by functional trait-based classifications to better predict ecological behavior. The analogous solution was to measure specific physiological phenotypes to create new groupings. Adapted to antibiotic therapy, the research would deconstruct the traditional categories by defining a set of key pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic and resistance-selection phenotypes for target infections. By empirically measuring where ampicillin and ceftriaxone fall within this multidimensional phenotypic space, the work aims to demonstrate that for specific, common clinical scenarios, they are functionally equivalent choices, arguing for a phenotype-driven rather than a spectrum-driven classification framework.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Vulnerability Screening to Identify Low-Risk Pediatric Patients
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in structural engineering—rapid seismic vulnerability screening of buildings—to the medical context of pediatric risk stratification. In earthquake engineering, rapid visual screening (RVS) uses a simplified, score-based system applied to key structural features (e.g., construction material, number of stories, presence of soft stories) to quickly triage buildings into low, moderate, and high collapse risk categories, efficiently directing detailed engineering analysis. Adapted to the original research seed, this involves developing a lightweight, interpretable machine learning model that functions as a clinical RVS tool. The model would be trained on a minimal set of key, readily available clinical and demographic variables (e.g., age, vital signs, basic lab results, specific symptom complexes) to rapidly stratify children with infection into risk categories. The primary output would be the reliable identification of a 'low seismic risk' equivalent: children at sufficiently low risk for treatment failure, making them safe candidates for initial narrow-spectrum antibiotic therapy. This approach conserves resources by applying complex, data-intensive models only to higher-risk patients, while providing a fast, transparent, and actionable decision aid for frontline clinicians managing low-risk cases.

ideas.title: Apply Econometric Causal Discovery Algorithms to Observational Antibiotic Data
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from econometrics, where causal discovery algorithms are used to infer causal structures from observational data, to the biomedical context of antibiotic research. The analogous econometric solution involves using algorithms (e.g., PC, FCI) to sift through high-dimensional observational data on markets or policies to identify plausible causal diagrams and valid instrumental variables. Adapted to this research, we would apply these causal discovery methods to rich observational healthcare data (e.g., EHRs containing patient vitals, lab results, comorbidities, physician IDs, hospital protocols). The goal is to systematically discover and validate potential instruments or adjustment sets for estimating antibiotic treatment effects, moving beyond manually chosen, single-variable propensity scores or instruments to a more data-driven, robust causal modeling approach.

ideas.title: Quantifying Exclusion-Induced Safety Bias: A Simulation Framework for Risk-Profile-Dependent Error Estimation
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a conceptual challenge to provide a quantitative tool for assessing the bias introduced by excluding early severe cases. It uses simulation studies to model realistic disease progression and patient risk heterogeneity, applying common exclusion rules to these synthetic cohorts. The output is a set of metrics quantifying the distortion in key safety endpoints and the increased risk of missing true safety signals, allowing for the development of evidence-based guidelines on when exclusion is statistically and clinically acceptable versus when it fundamentally compromises trial validity.

ideas.title: Assess vaccine impact as a 'protective buffer' against comorbidity-driven severity using effect modification analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from structural engineering, where retrofitting modifies the effect of structural vulnerabilities on collapse risk during an earthquake. The analogous problem in pediatric epidemiology is understanding how vaccination modifies the effect of comorbidities on hospitalization severity. By treating vaccination as a protective intervention that buffers against comorbidity-driven risk, the study will employ effect modification analysis to quantify the degree to which vaccination attenuates or nullifies the increased severity risk associated with specific pre-existing conditions in children, providing stratified, actionable data for clinical risk assessment and public health strategy.

ideas.title: Applying Seismic Aftershock Monitoring to Post-ED Discharge Surveillance
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that ED discharge equates to mild disease by drawing an analogy from geophysics. In seismology, significant aftershocks often follow the main earthquake but are not part of the initial assessment, solved through dedicated aftershock monitoring networks. Adapted to pediatric emergency medicine, this proposes implementing a structured post-discharge surveillance system for children sent home from the ED. Similar to seismic sensor arrays, this would use technology (automated check-ins, nurse callbacks, or wearable monitors) to actively monitor for delayed clinical deterioration or 'aftershock' symptoms, systematically identifying cases where significant illness was initially unrecognized during the ED evaluation.

ideas.title: A Multilevel Geospatial Framework to Disentangle Individual and Community Drivers of Hospitalization Severity
ideas.description: This research integrates individual patient data from EHRs with high-resolution environmental and socioeconomic datasets in a hierarchical Bayesian modeling framework. The system models hospitalization outcomes as a function of both individual clinical factors and contextual community exposures, while accounting for spatial dependence and variable healthcare access. This design explicitly tests for cross-level interactions, such as whether the effect of air pollution is stronger for individuals with specific comorbidities, to identify true, actionable community-level risk factors beyond aggregate correlations.

ideas.title: A Real-Time Equity Audit System: Using EHR Analytics to Deconstruct Racial Disparities as Signals of Systemic Failure
ideas.description: This research integrates the critical perspective that race is a social construct with the methodological power of EHR-based learning health systems. It develops real-time analytics to monitor clinical outcomes and guideline adherence, not by treating race as a causal biological risk factor, but by using racialized group differences as high-priority signals of potential systemic inequities in care delivery, resource allocation, or bias. The system continuously tests interventions (e.g., standardized order sets, bias training, community health worker integration) aimed at these systemic targets, measuring whether observed disparities diminish, which would provide empirical evidence against innate explanations. This creates a closed-loop audit and intervention platform that makes equity a measurable, actionable component of healthcare quality.

ideas.title: Calibrating ICU Severity Classification Using Multi-Center Instrument Calibration Methods
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of uniform hospital protocols by recognizing that variation in ICU admission thresholds acts as a systematic measurement bias, confounding severity classification—similar to how different uncalibrated instruments give biased readings in metrology. Drawing an analogy from metrology and instrument calibration, where the solution involves mapping varied instrument outputs to a shared reference standard via transfer functions, we adapt this to the ICU context. The adapted research idea is to develop a multi-center calibration framework: first, establish a detailed, high-fidelity reference measure of patient severity (e.g., a comprehensive physiological score from pre-admission data); second, conduct a study across hospitals with varied admission practices to collect paired data (local admission decision and reference score); third, use statistical or machine learning methods to derive hospital-specific calibration functions. These functions would adjust the locally observed 'admission threshold' signal, allowing for severity classification on a common, calibrated scale that accounts for institutional practice variation, thereby reducing confounding in comparative outcomes research.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Theory to Hospital Admissions: Identifying Non-Clinical Drivers of Hospitalization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that hospitalizations are solely disease-driven by drawing an analogy from ecological niche theory. In ecology, a species' presence in a habitat is determined not just by fundamental resources (like the clinical need for hospital care) but also by biotic pressures like competition (analogous to social or environmental pressures). The analogous solution involves modeling to separate these drivers. Adapted to healthcare, the research would frame the hospital as an institutional niche. The goal is to develop a multivariate model that includes standardized metrics for social vulnerability, caregiver availability, and home safety alongside traditional clinical severity indices. By analyzing admission data through this framework, the study aims to statistically partition the variance in hospitalization decisions, formally identifying and quantifying the proportion of admissions primarily attributable to non-clinical, social-environmental drivers, thereby validating the original challenge to the disease-only assumption.

ideas.title: Probabilistic, uncertainty-aware severity prediction from noisy, irregular time-series clinical data
ideas.description: This research develops a clinical risk stratification system that directly accounts for the noise, irregular sampling, and missingness of granular lab and vital sign data. Instead of treating raw values as precise inputs, the model learns to infer robust patient trajectories and quantify prediction uncertainty. The approach integrates data quality indicators and uses adversarial validation to ensure calibration across different hospital units and data collection practices, producing reliable risk estimates that communicate their own confidence to clinicians.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Risk Landscapes for Racial Severity Disparities in Pediatric Care
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution of ecological niche modeling from ecology to investigate racial severity disparities in hospitalized children. In ecology, models integrate diverse environmental pressures to map species vulnerability. Analogously, this study would model a pediatric 'risk landscape' by integrating multi-level data—including social determinants (e.g., neighborhood disadvantage, structural racism indices), biological factors (e.g., comorbid disease prevalence, genetic polymorphisms), and healthcare access variables (e.g., proximity to high-quality care, insurance type)—into a unified geospatial analytical framework. The goal is to move beyond listing individual factors to identifying the specific, cumulative combinations of interacting variables that create high-risk niches, thereby pinpointing actionable intervention points to mitigate severity disparities.

ideas.title: Adapting Industrial Statistical Process Control for Real-Time Clinical Guideline Monitoring
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from industrial Statistical Process Control (SPC) to the problem of real-time monitoring in a learning health system. In manufacturing, SPC uses real-time sensor data and control charts to monitor production quality and flag deviations immediately. The adapted research idea applies this framework to electronic health record data streams by defining clinical key process indicators for guideline adherence and outcomes, then implementing real-time statistical control charts. This system would continuously analyze EHR data, alerting clinical teams when metrics drift outside pre-established, statistically derived control limits, thereby enabling prompt investigation and corrective action to maintain care quality and fostering a true real-time learning cycle.

ideas.title: Applying Survivorship Bias Correction from Engineering to Clinical Safety Assessment
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that excluding early severe cases provides a fair safety comparison by drawing an analogy to survivorship bias in engineering and reliability analysis. In fields like aerospace, analyzing only systems that survived (e.g., planes that returned) gives a fatally flawed view of risks; the solution is to rigorously study all failures. Adapting this, the research proposes to treat early, severe adverse events in clinical trials not as outliers to be excluded, but as the most critical data points for understanding true risk profiles. The adapted idea is to develop a methodological framework that mandates the inclusion and systematic analysis of these 'severe failure' cases from the outset of any comparative safety assessment, using tools inspired by engineering's Failure Mode and Effects Analysis to properly account for their impact and prevent a biased, overly optimistic safety conclusion.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery-Informed Inference for Comorbidity Effects
ideas.description: This approach first employs constraint-based or functional causal model algorithms to learn a directed acyclic graph (DAG) representing the causal relationships among comorbidities, treatments, and outcomes from high-dimensional observational data. The inferred or partially-specified graph is then used to rigorously evaluate the feasibility of traditional quasi-experimental designs like IV or DiD by checking their required conditional independence assumptions. Where those designs are invalid, the system instead implements targeted estimation strategies, such as inverse probability weighting or g-computation, using the identified sufficient adjustment set from the causal graph to block backdoor paths. This creates a more principled and transparent pipeline for moving from associative data to defensible causal claims about comorbid disease interactions.

ideas.title: Adapting Conservation Biology's Habitat Suitability Modeling to Map Disease Severity Risk
ideas.description: This idea adapts the method of habitat suitability modeling from conservation biology to epidemiology. In ecology, models like MaxEnt analyze environmental variables against species location data to predict geographic suitability. Here, we apply the same core analytical framework to map community-level risk for severe disease outcomes. Geocoded hospitalization data (serving as 'occurrence points' tagged with severity) are analyzed against a suite of geospatial layers representing socioeconomic, environmental, and infrastructural variables. The resulting model identifies key risk factor combinations and generates a predictive map of 'severity risk suitability,' highlighting communities most vulnerable to severe hospitalizations to guide targeted public health interventions.

ideas.title: Apply causal inference methods from ecology to estimate comorbidity effects
ideas.description: This idea adapts causal inference methods from ecology, where researchers tackle confounding in observational systems using natural experiments and matching techniques. The analogous ecological solution involves using propensity score matching to estimate the causal effect of a disturbance (e.g., fire) by creating matched groups from observational data. Adapted to medical research, this approach would involve identifying a quasi-random source of variation in comorbidity acquisition (a natural experiment), such as a regional diagnostic guideline change, and then using matching methods to construct a valid counterfactual group. This allows for the estimation of the causal effect of the comorbidity on outcomes like treatment response or mortality, addressing confounding more robustly than standard regression adjustment.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Environmental and Genetic Variance in Health Disparities to Decouple Race from Biology
ideas.description: This research program employs a robust, multi-method design to empirically disentangle genetic from non-genetic contributions to racial health disparities. First, it uses variance partitioning in large biobanks to quantify the marginal explanatory power of continental genetic ancestry for a disparity after controlling for socioeconomic and environmental factors. Second, it develops polygenic scores for relevant traits within specific populations and tests their portability and predictive power for explaining between-group outcome gaps. The final phase uses causal inference and mediation modeling on longitudinal data to identify specific, modifiable environmental and systemic pathways (e.g., environmental toxins, access to care, discrimination stress) that account for the residual disparity not explained by genetics, providing actionable evidence for policy.

ideas.title: Refine severity prediction with dynamic, multi-scale feature fusion
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from meteorology, where multi-scale data assimilation (e.g., ensemble Kalman filters) dramatically improves forecast accuracy by fusing disparate data sources. The analogous clinical problem is improving risk stratification models that often use static, coarse inputs. The adapted solution is to develop a clinical data assimilation framework. This framework would continuously integrate granular, high-frequency data streams—such as vital signs, lab values, and potentially continuous monitoring data—into a core computational physiology model. The process dynamically corrects the model's 'patient state' estimate, enabling real-time refinement of severity predictions and reducing uncertainty, similar to how weather models assimilate local sensor data to improve short-term forecasts.

ideas.title: Applying the 'Canalization' Concept from Developmental Biology to Challenge Race as a Biological Variable
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that race is a biological variable by drawing an analogy from developmental biology's concept of 'canalization'—where robust developmental systems produce consistent phenotypes despite genetic or environmental perturbations. The analogous solution involves demonstrating that when developmental environments are controlled, phenotypic differences diminish, arguing against genetic determinism. Adapted to human health research, this proposes studies that rigorously match environmental and developmental contexts across socially defined racial groups. If health disparities (e.g., in birth weight, hypertension, or metabolic disease) disappear or significantly lessen under these matched conditions, it would powerfully demonstrate that the disparities reflect systemic inequities disrupting biological processes, not innate genetic susceptibility tied to race.
